question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's involvement to date in the (i) design and (ii) funding, of the planned new community centre in Broughshane.
answer_text: My Department has been aware of the Broughshane and District Community Association’s long-standing plans for a new Community Centre in Broughshane since 2018. My Department has not been directly involved in any design plans and to date has not had a suitable Departmental funding stream available for the group to access. I met with the Broughshane and District Community Association in July 2024 to hear about their aspirations for the Community Centre. Given that there were no Departmental funding streams available to support the project at that time, I asked my officials to signpost the group onto any potential alternative funding opportunities outside of the Department that may assist with their project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any steps he is taking to ensure that Departments are delivering value for money, particularly in the context of increased public spending pressures and budget constraints.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of each Minister in the first instance to ensure that spend by their department is delivered on a value for money basis and consistent with the budget agreed for their department.The Department of Finance makes guidance available to assist departments in undertaking this role. This includes Managing Public Money, Better Business Cases NI, and a range of other pieces of guidance. Business cases relating to spend above delegated limits also require Department of Finance approval.Looking ahead, I have asked departments to produce Five Year Plans as part of the wider Budget Sustainability Plan work. The purpose of these plans is to allow departments to set out their strategic direction and how they will go about achieving this within the available finances. Better value for taxpayers, particularly in the context of constrained budgets, can be achieved through the transformation of public service delivery. Transformation can improve the quality of public services and make the public sector more efficient and productive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what actions her Department will take to ensure it reaches its target of reducing the number of people killed in road collisions by at least 50 per cent by 2030.
answer_text: Road safety is a priority for me as Minister and I am committed to considering any initiatives that will improve road safety.  I recently announced an extensive package of road safety measures aimed at safeguarding schoolchildren travelling to and from school.  I am also taking forward a number of strategic interventions including a consultation seeking views on a change to the law surrounding the use of hand-held mobile phones while driving, a review of drink driving legislation and options to address drug driving as well as introducing graduated driver licensing.  I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on a number of speed related matters including the national speed limit on rural roads, HGV speed limits and 20mph speed limits in general after which I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise on this important issueAlongside this, I have asked officials to develop a new and forward-looking Road Safety Strategy Action Plan.  This is in partnership with our Road Safety stakeholders, who all work together to reduce the number of people killed in road collisions. While all of these measures I believe have the potential to deliver significant road safety benefits, we can all take immediate action to improve road safety to ensure that the 2030 target is met.  I believe we all have a part to play in reducing road deaths and it is our collective efforts that will make the difference going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what investments are being made to improve the infrastructure of rural roads; and (ii) how these investments will enhance road safety.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the rural road network through day-to-day Essential Maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, and also through a properly resourced programme of Structural Maintenance to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years, due to over a decade of austerity by the British Government.There is a rolling programme of resurfacing across the road network including rural roads and my Department is working hard to ensure that available funding is targeted at areas of greatest need. Over the last number of years my Department has also allocated dedicated funding to a Roads Recovery Fund, of which a significant proportion has historically been targeted to the rural road network. My officials have also been developing a new approach to road maintenance. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene. It will be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner. While work continues on bringing forward specific actions, the delivery of this new approach will be dependent on future budget settlements which have yet to be finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that 70 per cent of serious speeding collisions occur on rural roads, to detail what targeted strategies are being employed to address this issue.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively with partners to reduce death and serious injuries on our roads, to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Research shows that there is a correlation between speed and the frequency and severity of road collisions. Our system of speed limits is used to manage this risk in order to enhance road safety and reduce the likelihood of collisions. Speed limits vary according to the type of vehicle being driven and the nature of the road.   I would like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on a number of speed related matters including the national speed limit on rural roads, HGV speed limits and 20mph speed limits in general, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise on this important issue.Evidence shows that more than 95% of road deaths are due to human error, with causation factors such as careless or inattentive driving, inappropriate speed for the road or the conditions, and drink and drug driving accounting for most deaths and serious injuries.  NI Road Safety Partnership data available on the NIDirect website: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/ni-road-safety-partnership shows that excess speed was a contributory factor in one in seven of all fatal collisions in 2023.  While excess speed is not the main causation factor for the majority of collisions that result in people being killed on our roads, it clearly has an impact on the severity of those collisions.My Department’s approach to road safety engineering, including the provision of  speed control measures, is outlined in a number of documents that are publicly available to view at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/road-safety-engineering.  One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour, particularly to speeding. Drivers need to view speed limits as a limit, not a target, and drive at a speed appropriate to the road and surrounding conditions.  My Department’s public information campaigns repeat the message that speeding is not acceptable.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what works her Department is undertaking to improve road safety of rural roads, particularly in regards to hedges and overgrown grass verges.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the rural road network through day-to-day Essential Maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, and also through a properly resourced programme of Structural Maintenance to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years.There is a rolling programme of resurfacing across the road network including rural roads and my Department is working hard to ensure that available funding is targeted at areas of greatest need. Over the last number of years my Department has also allocated dedicated funding to a Roads Recovery Fund, of which a significant proportion has historically been targeted to the rural road network. My Department carries out regular routine maintenance inspections on all roads during which inspectors will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction, obscuring road signs, or have the potential to create danger for the road user. The priority given to undertaking remedial action will take account of the risk to road safety. My Department is committed to protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and its approach to verge management aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and weeds, while achieving a greater focus on environmental protection. By reducing the areas of verge cut on many roads to a single swath or, where possible without compromising on road safety, reducing to a single cut later in the season, it is possible to increase the focus on environmental benefit. Due to the extent of roadside verges to be maintained, my Department’s grass cutting season commences in April on a scheduled basis. The schedule includes sightlines across the network, and only those areas that can be left uncut without comprising road safety are unmown. Road safety is always our priority, and we continue to cut sight lines at rural junctions to ensure visibility for road users. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. My department will continue to promote the road safety public information campaigns and other road safety advice through www.sharetheroadtozero.com I would encourage everyone to share road safety messaging and to take the road safety pledge.

answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively with partners to reduce death and serious injuries on our roads, to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department plans to deliver education on (i) climate and (ii) renewable energy, topics in schools.
answer_text: Climate change and sustainable development are topics which are already covered at every level within the current Northern Ireland Curriculum.In addition, in November 2023, my Department commissioned the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) to greater embed sustainability and climate change education into the current curriculum by undertaking a Climate Change and Green Growth Project.  Key achievements to date include:A Climate Change Expert Group has been established comprising experts and stakeholders from the Department of Education, the Department for the Economy, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, Keeping Northern Ireland Beautiful, Queen’s University Belfast, Ulster University, and practitioners and other experts in this field. The development of a Sustainability Hub, available on CCEA’s website, to provide information and support for teachers, stakeholders and young people. The development of four videos which can be used to encourage discussion and critical reflection on the nature and role of Education for Sustainable Development across the curriculum. Members of the expert group also analysed a sample of CCEA qualifications to identify to what extent sustainability matters are covered in the specifications and assess the scope for further embedding of climate change, green growth and sustainability concepts.More broadly, the findings of the independent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum have just been published.  The report identifies significant weaknesses in the current curriculum, which had not been formally reviewed in over 17 years.  It recommends that my Department develops a new curriculum framework for Northern Ireland which has greater specificity about the important knowledge, concepts and skills that children should learn at each stage of their education.  I have established a Curriculum Taskforce to lead on this work who will work collaboratively with educators across Northern Ireland to translate the review’s recommendations into a practical, ambitious curriculum framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all analysis that was undertaken by his Department on the status of the various species making up the biosphere within Lough Neagh in the last twelve months.
answer_text: A range of assessments are carried out on species within the Lough Neagh ecosystem. Some are part of wider, rolling monitoring programmes, and may include work commissioned by the Department and carried out by external providers.Macrophytes and diatomsThe most recent Water Framework Classification was published in February 2024 and Lough Neagh was assessed as ‘Poor’ ecological status with both macrophytes (aquatic plants) and diatoms (microscopic algae) assessed as ‘Poor’ and Phytoplankton status assessed as ‘Moderate’ ecological status. Breeding and Wintering BirdsLough Neagh has been designated as Ramsar site, Special Protection Area and an Area of Special Scientific Interest, affording legal protections to the Lough and their component breeding, wintering and assemblage bird feature species. Bird species trends are mixed with some species declining and others increasing.NIEA participates in the ongoing British Trust for Ornithology (BTO)/RSPB/Joint Nature Conservation Committee (JNCC) Wetland Bird Survey (WeBS) monitoring programme. Lough Neagh is surveyed during the winter months and as of 2025, during the summer period also. The WeBS monitors internationally important non-breeding waterbirds, with waterbird indices and trends being reported annually. The latest UK report, published May 2025, is available at the following link and includes information on Lough Neagh.https://www.bto.org/sites/default/files/wituk-2023-24-report-bto-jncc-rspb-naturescot.pdfBreeding bird surveys incorporating tern and waterbird species across the Lough are undertaken by Lough Neagh Partnership with data being used to inform condition assessment of these key species features. Additional data on a range of scarce breeding species is provided by a range of eNGOs and volunteers.The breeding population status of great crested grebe and its distribution across the Lough is currently being surveyed and assessed by external contract. OtterNIEA and the Centre of Environmental Data and Recording (CEDaR) commissioned Queens University Belfast to undertake a survey of otters in Northern Ireland in 2024 as part of an all-Ireland survey of otter in 2023/24. This included surveys in the Neagh Bann River Basin District, specifically Lough Neagh. Fish research The statutory responsibility for the conservation, protection and management of salmon, eels and inland fish within Northern Ireland (excluding the Loughs Agency area), lies with DAERA.  The following reports are applicable to Lough Neagh.DAERA has commissioned AFBI to undertake scientific research on brown trout stocks within the DAERA area in Northern Ireland in 2024. This report provides an update on brown trout science undertaken at AFBI and builds upon a previous report published in 2011. https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/daera-area-brown-trout-status-report-and-research-updateDAERA has appointed an independent Standing Scientific Committee on Salmon (SSCS) to review stock status annually and advise on conservat

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department ensures that people, especially older people, get the full use of mental health services if they cannot use the My Care app.
answer_text: The MyCare app is an optional tool designed to give patients and service users secure access to parts of their health and social care records, such as clinic letters, medication information, and appointment details. However, it is not intended to replace traditional methods of communication or access to care.The Department of Health remains committed to ensuring that all individuals continue to receive full and equitable access to mental health services. Mental health care is delivered through a range of in-person, telephone, and written communication methods, ensuring that no one is excluded due to digital barriers.Where appropriate, MyCare also supports proxy access, allowing approved family members or carers to manage the app on behalf of the patient or service user, further supporting those who may need assistance with digital tools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what regulations are in place to oversee tour bus companies which originate from the Republic of Ireland and operate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Passenger-carrying transport operations originating from the EU operate under the Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA) or the Interbus Agreement and Protocol.   The TCA provided a temporary arrangement for bus and coach transport, with particular arrangements for travel on the island of Ireland, while the Interbus Agreement provides a formal arrangement to allow occasional journeys between the EU and non-EU countries.  Following Brexit, the UK became a contracting party to the Agreement.  In both the TCA and the Interbus Agreement, tour bus services fall within the definition of “occasional services”.   These occasional services are exempt from formal authorisation so long as they remain compliant with the requirements of the TCA or the Interbus Agreement.  Tour buses are required to carry prescribed documents during the journey which must be presented at the request of any authorised inspecting officer.   For the Interbus Agreement, these include a certified copy of the operator’s licence issued by the authorities of the State in which the operator is established, permitting them to carry out international transport services, and a control document which takes the form of a passenger waybill.    The TCA also requires carriage of a certified copy of the operator’s licence, but instead of a waybill the vehicle must carry a journey form, which is very similar in structure and content to the Interbus waybill.   Drivers of buses and coaches from operators established in Ireland undertaking occasional cross-border transport services are required to comply with laws and regulations in place in Northern Ireland.   This applies equally to operators established in Northern Ireland who carry out occasional passenger services in Ireland.  Enforcement and compliance checks on tour buses from Ireland are carried out by the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s enforcement branch.   Journey forms for Northern Ireland operators undertaking occasional passenger services in Ireland are also issued by DVA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of money tour bus companies, which originate from the Republic of Ireland and operate in Northern Ireland, contribute to the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: The North welcomes a huge volume of visitors annually travelling into the region via bus and coach. Tourism NI does not hold all of the data for this sub section of tourism which brings visitors here as part of all island itineraries, day trips and short overnight trips. There are many operators in this space. Tourism NI works strategically with ITOA (Inbound Tourism Operators Association Ireland). The relationship is part of a programme aimed at promoting the North to international travel trade. In 2024, ITOA members delivered 699,000 international visitors to Ireland. Visitors handled by ITOA members spent €640.2 million in Ireland. 10.3% of their visitors included the North on their trip.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what steps she is taking to ensure that apprenticeship funding is accessible to under-represented groups.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that apprenticeship opportunities are accessible, inclusive, and reflective of our wider society. This is crucial from both a social inclusion and an economic development perspective. To help remove financial barriers, the Department covers the full cost of off-the-job training for apprentices. Additional funding is available to support apprentices with disabilities. Under the Apprenticeships NI programme, a disability supplement of up to £2,886 is payable to training suppliers to help meet additional support needs. Specialist Disability Support Services are provided by a Disability Support Contractor, contracted by my Department to deliver specialised support to apprentices with a disability. Disability support is also available to support Higher Level Apprenticeship (HLA) provision. In this instance support is payable directly to Training Providers to help provide additional input of resources in terms of training time, equipment, or support for the participant to benefit fully from training. Further support is available through Department for Communities programmes, which can be accessed to help eligible apprentices secure and sustain employment. There are also a wide range of Sectoral Partnerships in place that enable employers and other stakeholders to develop a diverse range of apprenticeship pathways that reflect the opportunities within our economy. Looking forward, it is important to further develop our understanding of how we remove barriers to under-represented groups. This is why I recently launched the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund. The Fund specifically promotes apprenticeship uptake from three groups who are currently under-represented across apprenticeship programmes – women, individuals with a disability and individuals from areas of deprivation or disadvantage. The Fund has very intentionally brought together employers, educational institutions, voluntary and community organisations, and government. Working collaboratively, we can bring our collective expertise to the table to create pathways to success and supportive environments that enable individuals to thrive and reach their full potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 25156/22-27, to detail (i) the current number of staff that perform bee inspection duties; and (ii) what is the optimum number of staff to perform these duties.
answer_text: At present there are two Agriculture Inspectors who perform bee inspections as part of their overall Plant Health inspection duties. 		As the Department’s legislative responsibility is to respond to any confirmed outbreaks of specific foul brood diseases in honeybees, there is no defined staff capacity. As per my previous response to AQW 25156/22-27, the Department’s pool of Agriculture Inspectors, including those trained in bee inspections, has reduced recently. However, recruitment competitions are underway to increase the inspector capacity to ensure that the Department’s legislative obligations continue to be met, including responding to confirmed bee disease outbreaks. 	It is anticipated that increasing the number of inspectors to 4-5 within the Department, trained to undertake bee inspections as part of overall inspection duties, would provide greater flexibility in responding to outbreaks and improve resilience.		Contingencies are in place to deal with significant outbreaks, including the deployment of staff from across the Department and from other organisations if required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans she has to increase the funding available for apprenticeships, to meet the growing demand for skilled workers.
answer_text: My Department has funding responsibility for the provision of apprenticeship training in the North through the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes. This funding supports the ‘off the job’ training element of an apprenticeship with annual funding of approximately £45m.		Both programmes are demand led with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need. Funding is therefore distributed on a demand-led basis, reflective of need in industry sectors.  		Employer led Sectoral Partnerships are in place across the economy to ensure that the apprenticeship pathways and content meet the needs of that particular sector. For example, a new pathway is being developed within the Engineering apprenticeship framework specifically to meet the needs of the Food and Drink sector, a new Port Operations apprenticeship is being developed and ICT apprenticeships in Digital Skills for Business and Data Technician are under review.		Upon creation, ApprenticeshipsNI frameworks are allocated to the appropriate professional and technical funding group based on the infrastructure and resources likely to be required to deliver the framework. In addition, upon successful completion of the apprenticeship, the employer receives an incentive payment. This varies depending on the occupational area, ranging between £558 and £1,674.		Most recent published statistics show that there are 11,774 participants on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme and 2,520 on the Higher Level Apprenticeships programme.  Numbers continue to increase year on year, reflecting the Department’s commitment to apprenticeships as a career pathway and  the success of the current funding model.  One example of this is the significant increase in adult apprentices (all age apprenticeships) since the lifting of previous funding restrictions in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the measures in place to ensure that apprenticeship funding is distributed equitably across different sectors.
answer_text: There are a wide range of Sectoral Partnerships in place that enable employers and other stakeholders to develop and adjust apprenticeship pathways. Each Sectoral Partnership is supported by a secretariat with access to expert development partners. Approved apprenticeships are then delivered through a strong regional network of training providers that includes FE colleges, contracted private training organisations and our local universities. My Department also funds the ‘off the job’ training element of an apprenticeship with annual funding of approximately £45m.This funding is distributed in line with the the uptake of apprenticeships on a demand-led basis, reflective of need in sectors across our economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department monitors and evaluates the effectiveness of apprenticeship funding in achieving its intended outcomes.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeship Programmes are subject to economic appraisal from the outset, which include provision for monitoring and evaluation of outcomes against objectives to evaluate success and value for money. Expenditure is monitored and managed on a monthly basis. Each programme delivers against established Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in relation to retention, achievement and compliance. KPIs are monitored regularly through contract management processes and evaluated formally through mid and end point evaluation to determine any lessons learned and inform future policy direction. The Education and Training Inspectorate are commissioned by my Department each year to carry out a range of formal inspections, inspection related activities and evaluations for apprenticeship delivery through the ApprenticeshipNI programme.My Department has a network of Sectoral Partnerships in place whose main role is to ensure that employers, those in further education and other experts are central to the development of apprenticeship frameworks ensuring they meet the current and future needs of local participants and industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the allocation of funds for apprenticeship programs over the next fiscal year.
answer_text: My Department funds 100% of the cost of the directed, off-the-job, training element of an apprenticeship, spending approximately £45 million in the most recent year across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes. Below is a table outlining total expenditure on apprenticeships over the last 5 years and the current allocated budget for 2025/26: Financial YearTotal Spend (£)2020/202119,104,1352021/202224,218,7912022/202327,850,7912023/202440,639,0942024/202546,407,302** Provisional outturn figure for 2024/2025The allocated budget for 2025/2026 has still to be finalised.  The level of funding provided is determined by the specific apprenticeship undertaken and the associated level of qualification.My Department remains committed to working with the Executive to support future apprenticeship funding requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the current apprenticeship funding model addresses the needs of industries facing skill shortages.
answer_text: My Department has funding responsibility for the provision of apprenticeship training in the North through the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes. This funding supports the ‘off the job’ training element of an apprenticeship with annual funding of approximately £45m.		Both programmes are demand led with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need. Funding is therefore distributed on a demand-led basis, reflective of need in industry sectors.  		Employer led Sectoral Partnerships are in place across the economy to ensure that the apprenticeship pathways and content meet the needs of that particular sector. For example, a new pathway is being developed within the Engineering apprenticeship framework specifically to meet the needs of the Food and Drink sector, a new Port Operations apprenticeship is being developed and ICT apprenticeships in Digital Skills for Business and Data Technician are under review.		Upon creation, ApprenticeshipsNI frameworks are allocated to the appropriate professional and technical funding group based on the infrastructure and resources likely to be required to deliver the framework. In addition, upon successful completion of the apprenticeship, the employer receives an incentive payment. This varies depending on the occupational area, ranging between £558 and £1,674.		Most recent published statistics show that there are 11,774 participants on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme and 2,520 on the Higher Level Apprenticeships programme.  Numbers continue to increase year on year, reflecting the Department’s commitment to apprenticeships as a career pathway and  the success of the current funding model.  One example of this is the significant increase in adult apprentices (all age apprenticeships) since the lifting of previous funding restrictions in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) financial support; and (ii) administrative support provided by his Department to Mid and East Antrim Borough Council to meet the 70 per cent recycling target, in each year since the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 came into force.
answer_text: My department offers financial assistance to local councils to transform kerbside recycling, infrastructure and services through the Household Waste Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP). While the programme offers up to 50% match funding for successful applications, no financial assistance has been provided to Mid & East Antrim Borough Council since the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 came into force.DAERA has a strong record of engagement with all eleven local councils to ensure we are well-equipped to meet the ambitious recycling targets set in legislation.  Officials recently collaborated with Mid & East Borough Council to model a range of potential waste collection scenarios, designed to optimise waste collections and enhance recycling in the borough. My department provides funding to the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) to provide essential local council support, including specialist technical guidance and the development of the necessary evidence base for implementing change. The programme also includes collaborative efforts with local councils on key national and regional behaviour change campaigns through the National Communications Advisory Panel (NCAP), for example Recycle Week and Food Waste Action Week, designed to enhance the quality and quantity of recycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department has received any business case to upgrade the Martinstown bridge, County Antrim, with a secure path.
answer_text: The upgrade of Martinstown Bridge forms part of the planning conditions associated with the approval of a new primary school in the village. This proposal is being led and funded by the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail when her Department last reviewed the approach of local councils issuing parking fines; and (ii) whether her Department has a policy in relation to the fees associated with parking fines issued by private companies compared to local authorities.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for the enforcement of on-street parking across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of car parks owned privately or by local councils, nor the enforcement of companies that operate within them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work which has been conducted by his Department in evaluating the journey to school for the new Martinstown Primary School site, with specific relation to any upgrade to the Martinstown Bridge.
answer_text: The design of the new school build for Mary Queen of Peace Primary School, Martinstown, has been completed however planning approval conditions continue to be addressed by the project Integrated Consultant Team. The upgrade of the Skerrywater Bridge, Martinstown, to provide pedestrian access, was one of a number of planning approval conditions stipulated by Mid and East Antrim Borough Council. Collaboration with the Department of Infrastructure, Roads Division, is ongoing to ensure the necessary alterations to the bridge are made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans his Department has to introduce legislation to provide payment for the loss of electricity for homeowners following a serious storm.
answer_text: Officials from my Department meet regularly with the Utility Regulator to discuss progress on Electricity Guaranteed Standards of Service (GSS).The Utility Regulator have agreed to an action in the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2025 – ‘The NI Utility Regulator will consult on amendments to the Electricity Guaranteed Standards of Service, including in relation to incidents of adverse weather”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of financial challenges, to detail any initiatives which are being implemented to ensure the efficient use of funds in the procurement process within controlled schools.
answer_text: Before initiating any procurement activity, the Education Authority (EA) undertakes extensive engagement with key stakeholders, including school representatives, to ensure that the requirements accurately reflect schools’ needs. In parallel, the EA conducts comprehensive pre-market engagement with a broad range of suppliers to stimulate market interest ahead of the formal procurement process.In accordance with Procurement Policy Note (PPN) 04/21 Procurement Control Limits, all contracts and frameworks established by the EA are publicly advertised on eTendersNI and are subject to competitive tendering. For the Classroom Resources framework, each supplier provided a competitively priced list of core products aligned with school usage patterns. All suppliers underwent rigorous assessment to confirm their ability to deliver high-quality products at fixed prices, within agreed delivery timeframes, to all schools across Northern Ireland, ensuring consistency, fairness, and equal access.In addition, the framework was strategically designed to offer schools access to multiple suppliers, a competitively priced core product range, and extensive supplier catalogues.  The Classroom Resources framework resulted in the appointment of a broad range of suppliers (i.e. 17) and has delivered annual savings of £1.4 million.In alignment with public procurement policy and PPN 04/21, the EA ensures that all contracts and frameworks are publicly advertised on eTendersNI. This approach facilitates an open, fair, and transparent tendering process. The procurement process is structured to ensure competition and is underpinned by clearly defined evaluation criteria, ensuring that contracts are awarded based on value for money- defined as the optimal balance of cost, quality, and sustainability.The EA provides comprehensive advice and guidance to schools regarding the use of EA contracts and frameworks, including adherence to procurement control limits and procedures. This guidance is disseminated through multiple EA communication channels, including C2k and EA Procure, to ensure schools are well-informed and compliant with procurement requirements.Firstly, the EA conducts comprehensive pre-market engagement for all procurements to encourage maximum supplier participation and thereby more competitive tendering. This early engagement helps shape procurement strategies that are responsive to schools’ needs and market conditions.In addition, to ensure that procurement decisions reflect the practical needs of schools, tender evaluation panels include school representatives, seconded principals, and, where appropriate, former principals to better ensure that school-specific requirements are embedded throughout the procurement lifecycle.In addition, the EA continues to enhance the efficiency of the procurement and ordering process through digital innovation, including:Expansion of its iProcurement Online Shop: This has increased the range and accessibility of items available for

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to address the high number of classroom assistants employed on temporary contracts, many of whom have served for over four years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland.  The high number of classroom assistants employed on a temporary contract is undoubtedly a consequence of the current one-to-one classroom assistant support model.  This is because schools are unsure over the length of contract to offer a classroom assistant if there is uncertainty as to how long the pupil they are supporting will remain at the school. Whether a permanent or fixed-term contract is put in place depends on how long the pupil will remain in the same school. The EA advise schools to offer a permanent contract if it is expected that the role will be required for four years or more. Temporary staff who have accrued four years of service can contact the EA to have their application for a permanent contract progressed.There is no difference in the pay on offer for classroom assistants on permanent or temporary contracts.  However, temporary contracts do lead to uncertainty for the staff involved.The EA are currently reviewing the use of the Temporary Engagement process by schools with a view to restricting its use for posts that provide short term and emergency cover only.However, it is also clear that we need to critically evaluate the current Special Educational Needs support model with a view to developing a more responsive and flexible approach that provides the best possible support for our children.   In line with the Department of Education’s five-year Special Educational Needs and Disability Delivery Plan, the EA is progressing a strategic review of the classroom assistant employment model which includes job descriptions, contracts and a range of other employment-related matters including qualifications, professional standards and professional development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action that has been taken to protect the Glens of Antrim from wildfires.
answer_text: In 2022 DAERA consented 2 applications to undertake wildfire risk management at Slieveanorra and Croaghan ASSI within the Antrim Plateau.In January 2025 DAERA issued a procurement exercise which included the development of a wildfire plan for the Antrim Hills, but no applications were received. My officials are reviewing the process and will initiate a further procurement exercise as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what steps her Department is taking to tackle rural crime.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime, in all regions. The Department of Justice chairs the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP) with members including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Federation of Small Businesses, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The partnership shares information on emerging or reoccurring crimes, and will continue to do so, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing rural crime. RCP members regularly engage with the public via social media to raise awareness of rural crime issues and to encourage rural communities to report such crimes. At a local level, PCSPs, which are funded jointly by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in addressing crime, including agricultural crime, and building confidence locally, through engagement and consultation with communities, including rural areas. PCSPs play a pivotal role in supporting communities in identifying solutions to issues of local concern.  They also deliver a range of initiatives to raise awareness and support rural communities and businesses to protect their property and goods.  These initiatives include trailer/equipment marking events and such actions have helped to continue the downward trend in recorded rural and agricultural crime.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any engagement that they have had with the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland concerning the safeguarding of older people's rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following recent reports of toxic chemical pollution in the Irish Sea originating from the Isle of Man, to detail any monitoring being undertaken in terms of measuring the levels of these chemicals (i) in coastal waters; and (ii) in fish and other seafood caught in the Irish Sea.
answer_text: The Department undertakes monitoring of chemical pollutants as part of the requirements of the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, Marine Strategy Regulations 2010, and the OSPAR Convention for the Protection of the Marine Environment of the North-East Atlantic 1998.  This monitoring typically includes water, sediment, and biota (fish and shellfish).  Twenty-five coastal and transitional (estuarine) water bodies are identified under the Water Framework Regulations and water monitoring is conducted monthly at a representative site within each water body. Shellfish monitoring includes annual samples from seven water bodies as well as 15 sites within designated shellfish waters as part of a joint monitoring programme with the Food Standards Agency.  Fish are sampled from seven transitional waters and six sites within the Irish Sea, spanning four water bodies. Sediments are monitored at seven sites located in sea loughs and estuaries, and as part of licensed dredging operations and at dredged material disposal sites. Polychlorinated biphenols (PCBs) are monitored in sediments and in shellfish as part of the UK Marine Monitoring and Assessment Strategy (UKMMAS) and are reported under the UK Marine Strategy and OSPAR.  Monitoring results provided by the OSPAR Hazardous Substances Assessment Tool indicate that PCBs in sediment are generally below the ecotoxicological assessment criteria (EAC) while PCBs in shellfish are below the maximum permissible concentrations (MPC) for human consumption.  To date, fish collected in the Irish Sea have only been analysed for mercury and the results indicate that values generally exceed the Water Framework Regulations environmental quality standard (EQS) for biota but are below the MPC for human health.  Mercury is a ubiquitous, persistent, bioaccumulative and toxic (uPBT) legacy substance.  Additional analyses of uPBT chemicals are scheduled to take place in the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why the JobStart 50+ Scheme was discontinued, given the evidence of its benefits for older unemployed people.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. However, I was pleased to announce recently in the Assembly that I am investing in a new multi-million-pound employment programme that will support all age groups including those aged 50+.I will be making an announcement to the Assembly on the details of this programme in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered re-running or adapting the JobStart 50+ Scheme to build on its initial success.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. However, I was pleased to announce recently in the Assembly that I am investing in a new multi-million-pound employment programme that will support all age groups including those aged 50+.I will be making an announcement to the Assembly on the details of this programme in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is ensuring that older people are not disadvantaged by increases in National Minimum Wage and National Insurance in terms of employability.
answer_text: The impacts of National Minimum Wage and National Insurance rises in terms of employability in older people is not within the remit of the Department for Communities.  The Department for the Economy deal with skills and education and may have programmes specifically targeted at older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to support people over 50 years old to get back into work following the end of the JobStart 50+ Scheme.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. However, I was pleased to announce recently in the Assembly that I am investing in a new multi-million-pound employment programme that will support all age groups including those aged 50+.I will be making an announcement to the Assembly on the details of this programme in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to ensure continued access to cybersecurity readiness and digital inclusion funding for voluntary and community sector organisations in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Infrastructure Support Programme provides funding to the Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action (NICVA) to support the delivery of a range of direct support in relation to fund raising and governance, including cyber-security.Sectoral infrastructure support will continue to target funding towards the outcomes and priorities identified by the voluntary and community sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will commit to working with local stakeholders, including organisations like the Bytes Project, to co-design or support a regional cybersecurity funding model for charities working with children and young people.
answer_text: I recognise the vital role that charities working with children and young people play in our society, and I commend their efforts to keep those they support safe in an increasingly digital world. Significant work is already being undertaken by my department to strengthen our cyber security ecosystem. This includes investment in cyber skills development, collaboration with industry and academia through initiatives such as the Cyber Security Centre at Queen’s University Belfast, and support for innovation through our cyber security cluster, NI Cyber. Through these efforts, we are building a strong and resilient cyber security sector in the North - creating opportunities that can be accessed by organisations across the economy, including those in the voluntary and community sector. I am happy to consider any additional proposals to support cyber security.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will assume responsibility for the management and delivery of a Cyber Local funding scheme for 2025–26.
answer_text: Whilst I recognise the importance of such initiatives, no equivalent ring fenced funding has been made available by the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) to support a regional Cyber Local funding competition in this financial year.  		My officials continue to collaborate with DSIT and others to strengthen our position as a cyber security location of excellence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has made an assessment of the current capacity to manage a scheme equivalent to Cyber Local in Northern Ireland, including (i) digital infrastructure; (ii) application processes; and (iii) monitoring and evaluation.
answer_text: Introduction of an equivalent Cyber Local scheme for the North would require demonstration of need, in view of existing skills and innovation provision, alongside the requisite budget. My department in partnership with Invest NI will continue to engage with DSIT’s Cyber Lead in the North of Ireland to advocate for inclusion in any further competitions or iterations of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has had any engagement with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology in relation to the rationale for excluding Northern Ireland from the Cyber Local 2025–26 programme.
answer_text: The decision to fund only nine areas in England as part of the Cyber Local 2025-26 programme was made by the Department of Science, Innovation and Technology in London.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the reintroduction of a scheme similar to the JobStart 50+ scheme, considering the ongoing challenges for older people seeking employment.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support to help people, including those aged 50+, overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact the JobStart 50+ scheme has had on employment levels among those aged 50 and over.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ scheme was a small-scale test and learn pilot intended to help inform future policy for those aged 50-64 years old. Funding for the JobStart 50+ pilot allowed 180 participants to join the scheme with 160 completing their opportunity. I am pleased to advise that 83 (52%) participants moved into sustained employment either with their JobStart employer or with an alternative employer following their participation on the scheme.  An independent final evaluation of the JobStart 50+ pilot scheme is expected at the end of July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to address age-based discrimination in the workplace, particularly in recruitment and retention practices.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has legislative and policy responsibility for age-based discrimination in the workplace.  However, age-based discrimination in the workplace is also addressed by a number of DfE partner bodies.  The Labour Relations Agency (LRA) takes it lead from the Equality Commission on this topic to re-enforce LRA messaging about issues such as the language used in job adverts and work practices that could have a disproportionate impact on older or younger workers.  Complaints about age-based discrimination in the workplace can also be taken to the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement her Department has had with employers during the operation of the JobStart 50+ scheme, including any feedback received.
answer_text: My department engaged with employers at multiple points throughout the Scheme.  I am pleased to report that feedback gathered throughout the scheme demonstrated that an impressive 94% of employers positively rated their experience of the JobStart 50+ Scheme.An independent evaluation of the JobStart 50+ Scheme is underway and is expected by the end of July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of times (i) the Strategic Wildfires Group; and (ii) the Wildfire Stakeholders Forum established in 2021 have met, broken down by date.
answer_text: The NI Wildfires Strategic Group has met 7 times to date: on 15 March 2021; 27 September 2021; 02 February 2022; 10 June 2022; 27 June 2023; 15 December 2023 and 26 March 2024. The focus of these meetings was to develop a strategic way forward for addressing the issue of wildfires in Northern Ireland.The NI Wildfires Stakeholders Forum has met 3 times to date: on 14 January 2022; 07 March 2022 and 08 September 2022. The focus of these meetings was to seek feedback on proposals for a strategic way forward prior to a public consultation.I have asked officials to progress with the development of this strategic work and an associated action plan. This will involve re-engagement with these groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 25229/22-27, to detail why he is unable to provide details on the number of mould spore analyses carried out on mould in schools in the last three years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that they are not aware of any mould spore analyses being carried out in schools in the last three years. On being made aware of mould, EA Health and Safety would, in the first instance ask EA Maintenance to remove the visible mould and chemically treat the area. If the problem is persistent and the mould returns, they would recommend that an Occupational Hygienist is contracted to test the area where the mould is located for airborne spores and advise accordingly.These matters are under continuous review for remedial action, and should there be an immediate requirement for mould spore analysis at schools, this testing would be carried out in line with EA Health & Safety recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the Pre-School Education Programme system accounts for children from disadvantaged backgrounds; and (ii) any work being carried out to ensure they are not disadvantaged by limited or no availability of free playgroup places in the area they are from.
answer_text: Legislation set out in the Pre-School Education in Schools (Admissions Criteria) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1999 requires that all pre-school education providers prioritise children from Socially Disadvantaged Circumstances (SDC) before all other children in their admissions criteria.  The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for allocating sufficient pre-school education places to meet the aim of the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target aged child whose parents want it.  The EA continually reviews the levels of provision and demand in all areas and where additional funded places are required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings accordingly. The EA has advised that at the end of stage one of the pre-school admissions process, 97% children were in receipt of an offer of a funded pre-school place.  There are currently over 2,000 funded pre-school places available across Northern Ireland to accommodate children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department is carrying out to increase the number of special educational needs transition coordinators in their transition service.
answer_text: The responsibility for the transition service lies with the Education Authority (EA) who have provided the response below. A business paper outlining the need to create eight additional Special Educational Needs (SEN) Transition Co-ordinators and two specialist Transition Co-ordinators is being developed at present. This business case will be submitted to EA Senior Management for consideration by 2nd May.  Replacements for vacant posts are also being requested to fill vacancies that exist across the province.Transitions is an important element of SEN and the business case will outline the action plan for developing the reach and impact of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any action he is taking to address pre-schools that receive no or reduced Pre-School Education Programme places; and (ii) whether any consideration is being given to expanding the number of places available or to reallocate them more equitably.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in cooperation with the EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG), is responsible for setting Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory pre-school settings; my Department plays no role in this process.  The EA wrote to providers on 7 February to set out the process for the allocation of initial PANs, as well as the process followed in reviewing those allocations.  A copy of this letter was previously placed in the Assembly Library in response to AQW 24601/22-27. Each year, at least 99.8% children receive the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference by the end of stage two of the pre-school admissions process.  The second stage of the admissions process for the 2025-26 academic year is currently ongoing.  At close of stage one, 97% of children had already received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings accordingly.Nonetheless, I have requested that the EA review the PAN allocation process so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department monitors the fairness and effectiveness of the allocation process for pre-school places; and (ii) whether any independent reviews or audits are carried out to ensure that childrens' needs are being met equitably.
answer_text: The Department works with private and voluntary playgroup providers in a number of areas, such as the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) where approximately 330 non-statutory providers are funded to provide pre-school places for over 6000 pre-school pupils each year.  I have provided increased funding of approximately £3.2m to non-statutory settings participating in the PSEP in the 2024-25 academic year.  For an average non-statutory setting providing 16 funded pre-school places, this represents additional funding of £8,000 per annum.Within the Pre-school Standardisation Programme (PSP), the sector is represented on the Programme Board and non-statutory providers comprised 59 of the 106 settings I approved to deliver the standardised pre-school session length from September 2025. Private and voluntary playgroup providers may also apply to the Department’s Pathway Fund.  This provides a funding contribution towards the delivery of projects which support children aged 0-4 years, who are at risk of educational underachievement. In 2025-26 I have increased the maximum funding available from £30,000 to £40,000 and from £15,000 to £20,000 for Streams A and B respectively.  Voluntary and private playgroups can also access financial support for eligible working parents as part of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.  Through this scheme, working parents eligible for Tax Free Childcare can benefit from a 15% reduction in their childcare costs when their children attend participating playgroups.  Playgroups receive a payment to administer the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the recovery plan for the Mournes, announced in June 2021.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has no direct responsibility in relation to the Mourne Mountains.The then Innovation Lab, now part of Innovation & Consultancy Services in my Department, carried out research to identify key issues in relation to the area’s long-term sustainable future that need to be addressed. 		This research was completed and involved engaging with a range of stakeholders, individuals and organisations in the area. One of the key suggested actions emerging from the work was for an individual Executive Minister to take a leadership role in relation to the Mournes, who would then progress a Mournes Management Plan. 	Given the significant interests and responsibilities of the Department of Agriculture, Environment & Rural Affairs (DAERA) in the area, my officials shared the outcome of this work with colleagues in DAERA. I understand this is currently under consideration

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the potential (a) challenges; and (b) benefits of implementing a 30-hour free childcare policy in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how his Department plans to address these.
answer_text: As part of the work to develop an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, approaches adopted in other jurisdictions have been considered.  Initial evaluations of the 30 hour offer in England suggest it has helped parents increase their working hours and improved their finances, however, there have been access issues in some areas and funding challenges for providers.  My priority is to put in place a model of support in Northern Ireland which promotes child development, improves affordability for parents, supports sector sustainability and builds on existing infrastructure.  In September 2024, I implemented a Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) that has given eligible working parents a 15% reduction on their childcare bills.  Since the Scheme was introduced in September 2024, it has provided approximately £8million in savings for eligible working parents.  The scheme parameters can be adjusted based on the level of funding available.  I have also secured an Executive commitment to provide all children in the year before the start Primary school with 22.5 hours funded pre-school provision per week (currently available to just 40% of children).  I have approved an additional 106 settings to offer this from September 2025 and intend to roll it out further in subsequent years, subject to budget.  I have committed to bringing forward a comprehensive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.  This will set out an ambitious plan for the longer-term, including in relation of childcare affordability.  It will be for the Executive to decide on the level of funding it is prepared to allocate to this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what steps are being taken to accelerate road infrastructure improvements in North Antrim, including the A26 dual carriageway scheme.
answer_text: Regrettably the volume of requests for improvements to the road network greatly exceeds our capacity and therefore priorities must be established on how best to use our limited resources. Priority lists are prepared by my officials but frequently change due to factors such as new scheme proposals, new developments, land availability and available funding.Departmental officials are currently developing the 2025/26 Local Transport Safety Measures and Capital works programmes. Following confirmation of the budget settlement, details of the finalised programmes will be included in the local council annual reports. They will also be published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly Library after presentation to the councils.My officials are currently working on the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network (RSTNTP), which will set out investment priorities for the main road network, rail network and inter-urban bus routes for the next ten years.The A26 in North Antrim forms part of the strategic road network, and options for improvements will be considered while drafting the Transport Plan. At this stage, no decisions have been made in relation to the Plan.
