question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of community and voluntary organisations that applied to the Probation Board for Northern Ireland's Community and Voluntary Sector scheme, from September 2024 until its closure in March 2025, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department has worked in partnership with The Executive Office on the PSNI-led ‘Power to Change’ campaign that launched in January this year. As part of this, we provided funding to the PSNI to allow them to commission market research to inform the campaign. This research included interviews with key stakeholders and qualitive focus groups that explored public perceptions of and responses to different forms of gender-based violence and unwanted behaviours; as well as views on mindsets and attitudes that need to be challenged in Northern Ireland.The Power to Change campaign aims to tackle the underpinning behaviours and attitudes that can fuel sexual offending and gender-based violence, including violence against women and girls. EPPOC's latest 'Ending the Harm' campaign, which was launched in October 2024, highlights a number of ways that paramilitaries exploit and harm vulnerable people and communities. We know from academic research and programme insight the effect of paramilitary harm on women and girls. We also know that the coercion and intimidation that often comes with this makes victims feel that they have nowhere to turn to for help. To that end, one of the new campaign creatives specifically says "Paramilitaries abuse and control women and girls'. The intention is that, by highlighting the issue, we can encourage more victims to come forward for help and support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will introduce legislation to prevent registered sex offenders from changing their name.
answer_text: Under Part 2 (Notification and orders) of the UK-wide Sexual Offences Act 2003, registered sex offenders (RSOs) are required to notify the police of their use of a new name within three days of its use.However, a number of new proposals have been included in the Westminster Crime and Policing Bill (‘the Bill’), introduced in Parliament on 25 February 2025, which seek to strengthen and streamline the 2003 Act notification requirements and which I intend to extend to Northern Ireland by way of a Legislative Consent Motion, subject to the approval of the Executive and the Assembly. Two of these proposals relate to name change: the requirement to notify police no less than seven days before using the new name; and the requirement to seek authorisation of police before applying for a new specified identification document (immigration document, passport or driving licence) in a new name. Under the proposal to seek authorisation for a new identification document, police will consider whether restrictions are necessary to protect the public from sexual harm, and where risk has been identified, the police can refuse authorisation or cancel a previous authorisation. Should an RSO apply for a replacement document without authorisation, they will have committed an offence and will be liable on summary conviction to up to six months’ imprisonment, and on conviction on indictment, to imprisonment of up to five years.  Of the documents specified in the Bill, only a driving licence issued under Part 2 of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 falls within scope of devolved matters. For the proposals to be effective, there has to be the ability to refuse to issue a replacement driving licence where the relevant RSO has not sought, or been refused, the necessary authorisation. This ability is not available in current road traffic legislation across the UK. The Bill, as introduced, provides for regulations to be made by the Secretary of State for Transport to enable the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency in England, Wales and Scotland to refuse to issue a replacement driving licence. As the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) in Northern Ireland falls within the remit of the Department for Infrastructure, separate arrangements to provide for regulations to be made by the Minister for Infrastructure to enable the DVA to refuse an application will be required.  I am currently liaising with the Minister on this issue with the aim of having relevant provision included at a later stage in the progress of the Bill through Parliament.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to engage with the public on the renewable energy industry.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working collaboratively and engaging with our stakeholders across government, industry, domestic and business energy consumers to deliver the objectives of the Energy Strategy, including those in relation to renewable energy. Public consultations to date include the Design considerations for a Renewable Electricity Support Scheme, and the Draft Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan. In the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2025, my Department commits to establishing an energy communications group, led by the Consumer Council, to bring together key consumer stakeholders and partners to protect consumers and deliver information and advice to overcome the barriers and challenges that consumers face in actively participating and benefitting from the energy transition. In addition, a regular E-Bulletin has been produced since June 2020 to update stakeholders on the progress being made and work being delivered across a range of renewable energy areas, including offshore wind, biofuels, geothermal and the future electricity grid. The E-Bulletin is distributed via email to over 700 stakeholders and is publicly available on the DfE website - Northern Ireland Energy Strategy ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’ | Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of applications made for electric vehicle charge point grants in Northern Ireland in the last 12 months, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: There are a number of electric vehicle charge point grants provided by the Office of Zero Emissions Vehicles in the Department for Transport. These include charge point grants for renters and flat owners, landlords, staff and fleet car parks, workplace charging schemes, and state-funded education institutions.  Information relating to these grants, including numbers of applications is held by OZEV. They can be contacted at ChargePointGrantEnq@dvla.gov.uk or pluginvehiclegrant@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is evaluating the Skill Up programme.
answer_text: The Skill Up programme is continually monitored through monthly reports and compliance checks, supported by annual independent evaluations in line with the academic year. Each evaluation has reported a positive outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the effectiveness of the Prison Education Service for (i) increasing literacy; (ii) reducing reoffending; and (iii) increasing employment opportunities for ex-offenders.
answer_text: Research has shown that there are a number of social factors that contribute to offending and reoffending behaviour, including issues such as poor literacy, language and numeracy skills, and a history of unemployment.  Whilst it is not possible to precisely quantify the contribution that prison education makes to increasing literacy, reducing reoffending and increasing employment opportunities, the Prison Service works with partners to deliver high quality provision that improves the life chances of those in custody to encourage desistance from reoffending on release.In terms of literacy, the number of individuals self-reporting as being below Entry Level 3 is significant.  Such individuals are starting from a very low baseline, some of whom are likely to have left school before the age of 16 and are unlikely to have many qualifications.  This is a challenging context.  To encourage those individuals into learning, the Prison Service collaborates with voluntary organisations to offer non-classroom based support through peer mentoring schemes which provide basic literacy skills.  Accredited Essential Skills provision is offered from Entry Level 3 up to Level 2.  Over the past three years, there has been a slight decrease in Essential Skills Literacy qualifications at Entry Level 3 however there has been an increase at Level 1 and at Level 2.In addition to Essential Skills, the Prison Service offers individuals in custody the opportunity to attain skills and qualifications in economically relevant vocations (e.g construction, hospitality, hair and beauty) and offers employability skills training (e.g CV writing, interview techniques, communication at work) to help change behaviours, enhance employability and reduce the risk of reoffending.Significant investment has also been made to increase opportunities for employment on release, through engagement with employers across different industry sectors. Department for Communities Work Coaches are now working in all three prisons, supporting prisoners transitioning into the community. Since their introduction there have been 102 ‘warm handovers’ to Jobs and Benefits Offices and a total of 59 ex-prisoners have secured employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to help ensure that young people with special educational needs develop life skills.
answer_text: The Post 16 curriculum in Special Schools will generally include aspects related to the development of life skills, such as independence and social skills, online safety, travel training.  Mainstream Schools may do this to a lesser extent but it will depend on the profile of learners within their school.CCEA (Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment) qualifications in Preparation for Adult Life are available at Levels I and II.  The Specifications contribute to meeting the statutory requirements for the Learning for Life and Work Area of Learning at Key Stage 4Further information is available from CCEA Preparation for Adult Life and Learning Resources at SEN & Inclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, other than the provision of free school meals, to detail any steps his Department is taking to help support school-age children living in poverty.
answer_text: In addition to the provision of free school meals to over 90,000 pupils each day, my Department is responsible for a number of initiatives to provide support for children and their families living in poverty.In recognising that the costs of school uniform can be a significant financial burden to many families and those in poverty in particular, I have introduced legislation that seeks to make school uniforms more affordable for families. In addition, a school uniform grant is available to low-income families and provides a contribution towards the cost of a school uniform. In June 2024, I also raised the income threshold that applies to those in receipt of Universal Credit from £14,000 to £15,000 per annum in order to allow more families to be eligible for both free school meals and uniform grants.My Department continues to deliver the Extended Schools programme which enables those schools serving the most disadvantaged communities to provide a range of services and programmes outside the traditional school day. The programme aims to improve levels of educational achievement for disadvantaged children and young people by providing the additional support they might need in order to reach their full potential. Funding of £8.1m was allocated to this programme in the 2024/25 financial year. My department also provides funding for Full Service Schools based at the Boys’ Model and Model School for Girls in North Belfast and the Full Service Community Network in Ballymurphy due to the high levels of deprivation in these areas.In recognition of the additional challenges and costs involved in supporting children and young people from disadvantaged backgrounds, as well as those at risk of educational underachievement, my Department provides more than £70m of Targeting Social Need funding to schools each year as part of core school budgetsMy Department has been providing free period products for pupils in schools since September 2021 and is working closely with its ‘specified public service bodies’ to ensure the effective implementation of legislation on this provision, which extends to statutory youth settings.My Department is also working to deliver the RAISE programme, a new initiative which aims to raise achievement to reduce educational disadvantage by promoting a whole community approach to education through place-based partnerships. Collaboration with all relevant stakeholders is a key tenet of the programme’s design and a Locality Reference Group will be established in each RAISE locality. The Programme is continuing to be developed in collaboration with stakeholders across central and local government, the voluntary and community sector and schools. The investment will enable delivery of a bespoke local Strategic Area Plan for each place, codesigned by schools and other settings, families/carers, and others with valuable roles to play in supporting children’s learning in communities. The programme will run initially for 2 years and potentially exte

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20036/22-27, to detail the number of mould spore analyses carried out on mould in schools in the last three years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not currently hold this data.Matters relating to black mould are under continuous review for remedial action. Should there be a requirement for mould spore analysis at the schools identified, this testing is carried out in line with EA health and safety recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that received the one-off Pension Age Fuel Support Payment in March 2025 that were not eligible to receive it.
answer_text: To date my Department has not identified anyone receiving the one-off Pension Age Fuel Support Payment in March 2025 that was not eligible for a payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the eligibility criterion used to determine the groups selected to receive the £1500 support payment for voluntary and community organisations.
answer_text: Voluntary and Community Organisations currently funded in the 24/25 financial year by my Departments Voluntary and Community Division and Community Empowerment Division are eligible to claim the one-off support payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the work being carried out by his Department to support people with disabilities and long-term health conditions to get back into employment.
answer_text: During 2024/25, my Department allocated £28.9m to deliver a range of high quality employment support provision to address work related barriers, and help people move closer, into and progress in employment. Disabled people and people with health conditions are supported across this provision based on their unique and personal needs.This provision is delivered in partnership with a wide range of organisations, who also deliver their own supports.To address economic inactivity and sufficiently respond to the employability support needs of disabled people and people with health conditions, will require significant investment to greatly increase the capacity on existing provision, and to implement new provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline all measures being taken to allow the Department to plan for equitable healthcare provision across all communities.
answer_text: Reform is the only way we can ensure better and equitable access to healthcare for everyone. I have said before that the pressures evident in our health and social care system underscore the necessity for change. But it is not change for change’s sake. The goal is to ensure people have access to the right services at the right time, resulting in better outcomes.For example, last October I launched a consultation on how our hospitals could work better together. This means different hospitals will provide different services, and yes, people will need to travel to receive the best and equitable care. But we will have safer and more sustainable services as a result.Additionally, last December I set out a three-year plan to improve primary and hospital care, social care, productivity, and patient safety. Not only does the plan set ambitious goals to improve services such as Mental Health and Elective Care. It brings a stronger focus on reducing health inequalities.While I must not ignore the fact that the financial outlook is challenging, reform and having a three-year road map will help mitigate the impact of an inadequate budget.On a more technical front, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group carry out detailed equity comparisons of baseline funding, comparisons of quality, performance and service access indicators, to inform a strategy on the pace of change to address any gaps between fair shares and baseline investments.Finally, I would assure members that my Department and health and care providers have robust Equality and Rural Needs assessment processes to ensure any potential impact of policies or other decisions on people and rural areas are fully considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of youth work on improving employability and skills development and how it supports programmes that focus on promoting such work.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) Youth Insights Report - September 2021 to September 2024 reported that in the effective practice, young people understood and valued the transferable skills they acquired, through engagement in youth work programmes which prepared them well for their future education, employment opportunities and adult life.The Education Authority Youth Service (EAYS) has advised that in 2023-24   over 7,000 young people aged 14-18 years gained Level 1 or 2 Open College Network Northern Ireland accreditations in a range of programmes aimed to improve their employability and skills development.In addition to these accreditations, almost 70,000 children and young people engaged in youth work programmes specifically to develop their thinking skills leading to increased employability.  Furthermore, EAYS also supported 19 participants to complete an undergraduate degree in youth work leading to full-time employment within the Youth Service and a further 60 young people completed youth leadership programmes leading to employment as part-time youth workers.EAYS also administers a range of non-accredited and specialist programmes which develop valuable skills that contribute to the employability of children and young people. In 23/24 these included:Duke of Edinburgh Award programme for controlled schools and youth settings (delivered by EA Outdoor Learning Service - over 1300 young people);The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (skills based initiatives - over 200 young people);Horizons programme (in support of DE’s Fair Start Action Plan – over 200 young people).Youth Services deliver positive outcomes for children and young people including re-engagement with formal education, gaining qualifications and improving social skills, all of which contribute to increased employment prospects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure young people with special educational needs can access work experience opportunities in a variety of sectors.
answer_text: Work experience is an opportunity for planned and appropriate experiences of the world of work to increase learners’ motivation, develop their employability skills and make their learning relevant.  Work-related learning is a core component of a high quality CEIAG programme and all learners, irrespective of ability or background, should have equal opportunity during post-primary school to experience a number of planned and appropriate work placements.Schools are keen to avail of work experience opportunities for their learners and welcome proactive engagement by employers.  Whilst work experience is an operational matter for the Education Authority, officials in my department are happy to disseminate relevant information about work experience opportunities to the network of careers teachers, co-ordinators, including those in the special school sector. More generally, businesses and employers have an important role to play in supporting schools through not only work experience, but also through industry challenges, visits and participation in careers fairs.  I have seen many examples of effective collaboration between schools and employers and both I and my officials will continue to engage with businesses and other stakeholders to ensure that these opportunities are accessible to all learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to ensure the cost for new energy connections in Northern Ireland is fair for homeowners and developers.
answer_text: Fair and reasonable energy connection charges are essential for the decarbonisation targets, economic development, and a just transition for the people of the North. My department is currently consulting on proposals to socialise some elements of connection charges. The intended consequence of the preferred option in this consultation is to make grid connection charges fairer and more affordable, particularly for those living in areas where the electricity network is weaker, usually in rural areas. The consultation will close on 24 April and implementation of any changes is targeted for the end of the year. NIE Networks has introduced contestability in the works required for connections in the distribution network and in the transmission network.  This means that persons seeking to connect to the electricity network can use their own contractors.  Only steps which directly affect network security are reserved for NIE Networks. NIE Networks’ connection charges are based on the unit costs approved by the independent Utility Regulator and published by NIE Networks.  The Utility Regulator is responsible for regulating the gas and electricity industries in the North, promoting the short- and long-term interests of consumers. The Utility Regulator’s Price Control for local gas distribution companies allows for households in the North to be provided with a free connection and gas meter where natural gas is available.  Offers, which vary by region, are also available to assist householders with the cost of installing a gas boiler, including 0% interest free credit, cashback offers for switching from oil to natural gas, and funding from the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) to help convert low-income households to natural gas central heating.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department plans to introduce competition in the energy market, given that NIE Networks currently has a monopoly on new energy connections.
answer_text: There are a number of mechanisms, introduced by the Utility Regulator in 2015, which promote competition in new electricity connections - Decision paper - contestability in electricity connections | Utility Regulator.  When a customer applies for a new connection, NIE Networks will provide a dual offer containing two types of work:Contestable Works: Connection works that can be carried out by NIE Networks or by an accredited Independent Connections Provider (ICP). Customers have a choice when deciding who shall complete these elements of their connection.Non-Contestable Works: Connection works that can only be carried out by NIE Networks for the safety and security of the distribution network. Customers cannot choose who completes these elements of the connection - it must be NIE Networks.The NIE Networks website https://www.nienetworks.co.uk/connections/competition-in-connectionslists contestable and non-contestable works, and provides a link to an Independent Connection Provider Portal - https://www.nienetworks.co.uk/icp which contains detailed technical specifications and supporting design and build information for electricity connections.Consumers who wish to have a new connection can engage with NIE Networks in regard to their particular connection - Get in touch with our Customer Services team about our connection services | Northern Ireland Electricity Networks

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department is taking to retrieve any payments that have been made to people not eligible for the one-off Pension Age Fuel Support Payment in March 2025.
answer_text: Where an overpayment is identified, my Department will seek to recover where appropriate in line with Departmental policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that received an overpayment from the one-off Pension Age Fuel Support Payment in March 2025.
answer_text: Pension Age Fuel Support Payments commenced from 21 March 2025. A dedicated team is currently handling any cases emerging where an adjustment in payment is required. Information relating to the number of cases where an overpayment arose will be available when all payment queries have been resolved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps his Department is taking to increase the regulation of private car parking companies.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement, it has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to support special educational needs transport providers with the cost of increased employers' National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: School transport is one of many vital services provided by private sector employers on behalf of the public sector and I am concerned about the impact the recent Budget may have on the cost to employers of providing those services. The impact on employers of the increase in National Insurance Contributions (NIC) will vary depending on the size of the business, how many it employs and how it manages the new arrangements. I understand that a number of the private bus and taxi operators, who hold contracts with the Education Authority (EA) to provide school transport for pupils with special educational needs or disability, are smaller businesses whose NIC liability may not increase or are self-employed and therefore unaffected. The EA does not, however, hold information that would allow it to identify the private bus and taxi operators who will be impacted or to make a detailed assessment of the extent to which this may increase their operating costs. The EA works closely with its transport operators and has advised that to date none have raised concerns about the rise in operating costs due to the increase in NICs, however it will engage with any who are impacted and for whom this presents difficulties.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer (i) AQW 23562/22-27; (ii) AQW 23563/22-27; and (iii) AQW 23564/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 23562/22-27 was answered on 31 March 2025.AQW 23563/22-27 was answered on 11 April 2025.AQW 23564/22-27 was answered on 23 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what steps she is taking to reduce the number of people, below the age of 35, who are economically inactive.
answer_text: Question 11 AQO 1820/22-27My department is focused on ensuring that everyone in our society has access to skills pathways to access Good Jobs. This includes people under the age of 35 who are economically excluded.Although the loss of EU funding has significantly reduced the support to people who face barriers to employment, my department delivers a range of support, including Step Up, Skill Up and Skills for Life and Work, NI Apprenticeship Programme and an all-age careers service. The Apprenticeship Challenge Fund promotes apprenticeship uptake from women, individuals with a disability and individuals from areas of deprivation.My Department has also supported the Business in the Community Open Doors Inclusive Recruitment Campaign, aimed at employers to support them to extend recruitment practices to reach more people who face barriers to entering the labour market.My department works closely with the Department for Communities who have lead responsibility for employability. Officials are actively engaged in the development of the Disability and Work Strategy and work directly with the Labour Market Partnerships to deliver local solutions.We also work closely with the Departments of Health and Education on “A Life Deserved Strategy” which aims to help care experienced children and young people achieve their full potential and improve their outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process for facilitating the transfer of a pupil to an alternative school, where their current school environment is unsafe due to bullying and harassment.
answer_text: Bullying in our schools, for whatever reason and in whatever form, is always unacceptable. The Department of Education (DE) is committed to tackling bullying in schools and supporting all our pupils. To that end, DE introduced the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, which came into effect in 2021, and the Education Authority’s (EA) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) was subsequently established in 2022 to support schools to provide parents/carers and young people with support to deal with incidents of bullying.Although all efforts should be made to deal with instances of bullying type behaviour in such a way that negates the need for children to transfer school, regrettably, on occasions, this may be the outcome. The EA is responsible for school admissions and will be able to assist in the search for a suitable school, provide information on the process of gaining entry into schools and the documentation required. EA’s website also includes a search facility to locate schools in the wider area.  Depending on which schools are of interest, contact should be made with each school to establish if they have places available. Schools can only offer places if they have available spaces. However, if a school refuses admission and there are available places within the school’s approved enrolment number, the Department can take forward an admissions complaint on behalf of the applicant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of convictions for sheep worrying in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence in relation to sheep worrying.  Such offences may be recorded under offence descriptions relating to dogs worrying or attacking livestock, prosecuted under the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983.  In the years 2020 – 2024, there were a total of 12 convictions at courts for these offences.  Departmental convictions datasets do not contain details of the type of livestock worried or attacked, so the figures provided may not relate specifically to cases where sheep were involved. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is leading on a scoping exercise to better understand the challenges to sustainability that childcare providers face and identify an appropriate government response.
answer_text: Whilst childcare policy is primarily the responsibility of the Department of Education, delivering ‘more affordable, accessible, high-quality Early Learning and Childcare’ is an Executive priority in the Programme for Government. My officials have commissioned research to determine specific business needs of childcare providers and an appropriate response.  This research is nearing completion, and the resultant evidence base will allow me to move quickly to make recommendations to the Education Minister on the nature of assistance required by childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the organisations or individuals they engaged with prior to drafting the job description for the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and Ulster British tradition.
answer_text: The competition documentation including the role description, person specification, essential criteria and selection process was subject to the views of the selection panel, which includes a member of the stakeholder community with a background in the subject area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20036/22-27, to detail (i) the level of spores found per cubic metre; and (ii) the fungal classifications the spores originated from, for each of the 84 schools listed.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not currently hold this data.Matters relating to black mould are under continuous review for remedial action. Should there be a requirement for mould spore analysis at the schools identified, this testing is carried out in line with EA health and safety recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to make financial education a mandatory part of the curriculum for all students aged 5 to 18.
answer_text: Financial capability, including money management and savings, is already embedded as a statutory and integral element of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  Opportunities for the teaching and learning of financial capability occur across the curriculum at all key stages and through young people’s everyday experiences inside and outside school.  The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides a range of support and guidance to schools and has worked closely with other organisations to promote financial capability and to help produce resources for teachers to use.  An example of this is the ‘Your Money Matters’ textbook and teachers’ guide that was produced by Young Enterprise Northern Ireland and co-funded by Martin Lewis OBE and the Money and Pensions Service.  This resource, which was made available to every post-primary school, was designed for use with young people aged 14 to 16 and covers topics including spending and saving, borrowing, debt, insurance, student finance and future planning.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they had with Ulster Scots organisations prior to drafting the job description for a Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.
answer_text: The competition documentation including the role description, person specification, essential criteria and selection process was subject to the views of the selection panel, which includes a member of the stakeholder community with a background in the subject area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any plans they have to take forward legislation to provide a payment for the loss of electricity for home owners following a serious storm.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy is taking the lead on this issue so it would be appropriate for them to respond.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have tasked an Executive colleague to bring forward legislation to provide a payment for the loss of electricity for home owners following a serious storm.
answer_text: Energy Policy does not sit within the remit of The Executive Office, it would therefore be appropriate for the Department for the Economy to respond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the action she will take to improve the cycling infrastructure around Ballymena Bus and Train Station.
answer_text: The delivery of future active travel improvements will be primarily based on my department’s Active Travel Delivery Plan. Public consultation on the draft plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are currently reviewing the comments received as part that consultation. I hope to be in a position to present the final plan in the autumn.The plan details draft network plans for our largest towns and cities, including Ballymena. Routes to key destinations including schools, transport interchanges and town centres have been priortised with the aim of encouraging people to consider a behavioural shift to  walking, wheeling or cycling for many of their shorter everyday journeys.The Active Travel Delivery Plan does not specify the exact infrastructure required along each route.  This will be developed through the design of the individual projects that are taken forward from the plan, and further public engagement will take place on a project specific basis with the aim of creating infrastructure that works for users, and for the community.

answer_text: My Department allocates a budget to operate Movanagher Fish Farm, which provides stocking of certain Public Angling Estate waters, and to operate the River Bush Salmon Station, which only provides stocking of salmon to the River Bush. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is encouraging male representation in early years and lower primary school levels, as recommended in the Fair Start report.
answer_text: Research has shown that academic motivation and engagement are not significantly influenced by teacher gender, with no clear advantage for boys being taught by male rather than female teachers. It is the quality and effectiveness of teaching that has the greatest impact on pupils’ outcomes and wellbeing.  The priority, therefore, is to ensure that teaching in Northern Ireland remains an attractive and accessible profession for high-quality graduates.Nonetheless, my Department, along with teacher employers, recognises the importance of a teaching workforce in Northern Ireland that is reflective of society as a whole.  Increasing the number of males entering the teaching profession is, therefore, an ongoing priority for our Higher Education Institutions (HEIs), which deliver Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes. In recent years, HEIs have taken specific steps to attract more male applicants, including ensuring there is equal gender representation and a balanced presence of male and female students when promoting teaching as a career, for example on publicity materials and when attending face-to-face information events or school visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department will use data and research to make informed decisions, monitor programmes and evaluate spending.
answer_text: My commitment to ensuring that decisions are evidence informed is reflected in my recently published education strategy TransformED, which uses data and research to highlight the current context and challenges facing our education system. The strategy specifically recognises the importance of a research function that will ensure a strong evidence-base for future education policy and practice in Northern Ireland.	Data and research shape the policies, action plans and practices that improve learning outcomes, better support teaching and learning and create a culture of continuous improvement within the education system.We use data to monitor policies and interventions, so we know if they are having the intended impact and when to intervene. As well as ongoing monitoring of delivery of interventions, data and research is used to evaluate policy to measure outcomes, determine value for money and to learn lessons. Effective decision making requires a comprehensive evidence base comprising data which sets out the current context alongside local and international research evidence.The Department’s evidence base is assessed on an annual basis as part of the Department’s analytical work programme. This is overseen by the Data Strategy Group which comprises policy officials and analysts. It was established to drive a culture of evidence informed policy and practice across the system, ensuring that resources are directed toward the highest priority research and analysis needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any support he can provide to the Department of Education in terms of funding and to ensure that Northern Ireland can provide more affordable, accessible, high-quality early learning and childcare opportunities, as stated in the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: ANSWERIn the Executive’s 2024-25 Budget, £25 million was provided for a range of support measures in respect of early learning and childcare.This included the local Childcare Subsidy Scheme, enabling eligible parents to receive a 15% discount off their childcare bills (in addition to Tax-Free Childcare savings) where their provider is participating in the scheme.  The most up-to-date information is that over 14,000 children have been registered for the scheme, reducing families’ bills on childcare by £6.5 million since its launch in September 2024.A further £50 million has been allocated to early learning and support measures in the draft budget for 2025-26, and it is for the Education Minister to seek Executive agreement on his proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is ensuring that Initial Teacher Education and Continuing Professional Development are up-to-date and meet the needs of teachers in supporting disadvantaged learners.
answer_text: High-quality teachers are the most important feature of a successful education system. As outlined in my Department’s Education Strategy, TransformED, the focus is therefore on investment in and supporting high-quality initial teacher education (ITE) and continuing professional development (CPD).   This includes supporting teachers to meet the needs of disadvantaged learners.  I will be considering further the approach to both ITE and CPD in the coming months. There are key actions within TransformED to develop a core content framework for ITE to provide a structure and evidence-based foundation for training new teachers, including in supporting disadvantaged learners and also to ensure there is sustained investment to provide a coherent approach to Teacher Professional Learning that supports teachers throughout their career.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how often her Department (i) surveys for litter; and (ii) removes litter from Department of Infrastructure designated roads in the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council area.
answer_text: Article 7(2) of the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 requires that my Department ensures that any road which is not a relevant road (e.g. a motorway) is kept clean, so far as is practicable.Within Mid & East Antrim this applies to the M2 Ballymena bypass. This road is subject to two road closures during the summer months to complete safety related maintenance activities.  In advance of these closures, officials will determine if a litter pick is required and action appropriately.If should also be noted, that under the same Order, there is a duty on councils, to ensure that any relevant road for which it is responsible (i.e. all other publicly maintained roads within their district), is kept clean.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any lessons learnt and implemented from the last avian influenza outbreak.
answer_text: The last avian influenza outbreak in Northern Ireland was a single incursion of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza H5N1 at the captive bird establishment Castle Espie in County Down in October 2022. This single HPAI incursion did not require a debrief with a lessons learnt process as no significant issues were identified during this disease eradication operation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the cost to his Department of the last avian influenza outbreak.
answer_text: The last avian influenza outbreak in Northern Ireland was an incursion of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza H5N1 at the captive bird establishment Castle Espie in County Down in October 2022. The total cost to the Department linked to this outbreak was £495.60. This does not include business as usual costs, for example staff resource during normal working hours.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of day care facilities having to increase their rates in April 2025, potentially negating any benefits provided through the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme and Tax Free Childcare top up, to detail any assistance he is providing these facilities to ensure they do not have to increase their rates again and impacting parents struggling to pay for childcare and continuing to work.
answer_text: I am aware of price increases facing parents accessing childcare.  My Department does not have a role in regulating childcare fees for a sector which is largely made up of private providers, however, I am reviewing the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) parameters to ensure that it remains effective in reducing childcare costs for working families. I have already announced that the NICSS will continue into 2025/26 and the subsidy cap, which is the maximum monthly amount that can be claimed per child, will be increased by 10% to reflect the anticipated rise in childcare fees from April 2025.  I have also raised the administrative payment paid to providers delivering the scheme to ensure that any additional operating costs associated with its implementation are not passed on to parents.	I am considering what further enhancements would be affordable within the allocation I have secured in the draft Executive budget for 2025-26.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues.In terms of the sustainability of childcare providers, the Department for the Economy is leading on a scoping exercise to better understand the challenges they face and identify an appropriate government response. All the issues you have raised are being considered as part of the ongoing work to develop an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I intend to bring forward for Executive consideration and public consultation in autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department is planning to review the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to assess its effectiveness in light of ongoing price increases; and (ii) any plans to adjust the scheme to better reflect the true cost of childcare for families.
answer_text: I am aware of price increases facing parents accessing childcare.  My Department does not have a role in regulating childcare fees for a sector which is largely made up of private providers, however, I am reviewing the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) parameters to ensure that it remains effective in reducing childcare costs for working families. I have already announced that the NICSS will continue into 2025/26 and the subsidy cap, which is the maximum monthly amount that can be claimed per child, will be increased by 10% to reflect the anticipated rise in childcare fees from April 2025.  I have also raised the administrative payment paid to providers delivering the scheme to ensure that any additional operating costs associated with its implementation are not passed on to parents.	I am considering what further enhancements would be affordable within the allocation I have secured in the draft Executive budget for 2025-26.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues.In terms of the sustainability of childcare providers, the Department for the Economy is leading on a scoping exercise to better understand the challenges they face and identify an appropriate government response. All the issues you have raised are being considered as part of the ongoing work to develop an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I intend to bring forward for Executive consideration and public consultation in autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the People’s Postcode Lottery in relation to their ability to operate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 16308/22-27. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any collaborations between her Department and the Department for Communities to enhance career pathways into the museum and heritage sectors.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with the Department for Communities on the Heritage, Culture and Creativity Contact Group. A key aim of our apprenticeship provision is to provide high quality pathways into careers across our economy. My department is keen to explore any opportunities in the museum and heritage sectors and collaborate with our network of sectoral partnerships to ensure we have suitable pathways in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any efforts being made to promote museum work experience opportunities to (i) schools; (ii) colleges; and (iii) universities to encourage young people to consider careers in heritage and culture.
answer_text: The promotion of work experience and placements falls within the remit of the Department of Education (DE) and not my Department, although I recognise the value that these opportunities can bring to young people. My officials have been working closely with colleagues in DE to produce a joint Careers Action Plan, which will examine ways for learners to engage with employers and the world of work. This will include support for schools to enable a minimum work experience offer, including exposure to growing and emerging sectors.  The Action Plan is expected to be published in the coming weeks. Furthermore, my Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised advice and guidance on all sectors, including heritage and museums. This is provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers, on an all-age basis. The Careers Service also provides an online Careers A-Z tool, offering instant and flexible access to up-to-date information on careers, skills, labour market information and qualifications, to help inform and develop the career planning and management of young people and adults.  The A-Z tool includes information on museum-based careers, and other related roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures in place to ensure work experience opportunities in museums are fairly compensated and not limited to unpaid volunteer roles.
answer_text: The National Museums NI Chief Executive has responsibility for staffing matters and for determining the staffing structure to both meet operational needs and carry out its statutory functions. 	Source: National Museums NI Partnership Agreement with DfC	https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/partnership-agreement-between-department-communities-and-national-museums-ni

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any available funding and grant schemes available to help museums offer more placements, internships and apprenticeships for people seeking to gain hands-on experience in the sector.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI programme provides funded apprenticeship training at Levels 2 and 3 across a wide range of sectors. An incentive payment ranging from £558 - £1,674, depending on the occupational area, is payable to the employer upon successful completion of the apprenticeship. Previously, the ApprenticeshipsNI programme offered apprenticeships at Level 2 and 3 in Community Arts, Cultural Heritage, and Cultural Venue Operations.  However, these frameworks are not currently offered due to a combination of no apparent demand from the sector and expiry of the underpinning qualifications.  Should employer demand become apparent, the Department for the Economy-supported Creative and Cultural Sectoral Partnership will work with the sector to refresh the frameworks and source relevant qualifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps her Department is taking to support individuals seeking work experience in museums.
answer_text: Work experience is recognised as having a key role in ensuring learners are provided with relevant labour market information on current and future employment opportunities.  It is an opportunity for planned and appropriate experiences of the world of work to increase learners motivation, develop their employability skills and make their learning relevant.  Work related learning is a core component of a high quality CEIAG programme and all learners, irrespective of ability or background, should have equal opportunity during post-primary school to experience a number of planned and appropriate work placements.Schools are keen to avail of work experience opportunities for their learners and welcome proactive engagement by employers.  Whilst work experience is an operational matter for the Education Authority, officials in my department are happy to disseminate relevant information about work experience opportunities to the network of careers teachers and the Careers Service. More generally, businesses / employers have an important role to play in supporting schools through not only work experience, but also through industry challenges, visits and participation in careers fairs.  I have seen many examples of effective collaboration between schools and employers and both I and my officials will continue to engage with businesses and other stakeholders to ensure that these opportunities are accessible to all learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement with the UK Government to ensure the ready supply of Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy treatments.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the other devolved governments, including my Department, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA), and others within the supply chain to put in place well-established tools and processes if a supply disruption does occur, to ensure patients can continue to have access to appropriate treatments.My Department attends UK-wide and local medicine shortage management groups and is aware of supply disruptions affecting pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) products. Through these groups, my Department has been engaging with DHSC and has implemented UK-wide mitigations to ensure patients can continue to have access to appropriate treatments. This has included communication to healthcare professionals via National Patient Safety Alerts and the use of the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) medicines supply tool; addition of affected products to the list which prevents the hoarding and exporting of these products from the UK, and the use of Serious Shortage Protocols (SSPs) that permit pharmacists to limit the supply of these products to ensure all patients can have access to the limited supplies available.Additionally, I recently wrote to the Secretary of State for Health, Wes Streeting MP and the Minister of State for Health (and Secondary Care), Karin Smyth MP, highlighting the impact medicine shortages, including PERT products, is having on patients and services in Northern Ireland. I have also highlighted the need for continued collaboration between our Departments and the other devolved governments to explore options to improve the resilience of medicine supply chains to the UK.

answer_text: The aim of all international engagement is to promote our region as a place where people want to live, to visit, to study and invest, and to build relationships that can help us deliver the Executives objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will engage with the independent figure, appointed by the British Government and Irish Government, to examine whether a formal process of engagement with paramilitary groups is required to bring about their disbandment.
answer_text: To date the UK and Irish Governments have not published any details regarding the interlocutor, or the exact nature of the work that will be undertaken by them. It would be premature of me to make any commitments regarding engagement until further clarity is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether Northern Ireland will be part of the bovine TB on farm vaccine trials; and (ii) whether input from the EU required for them to proceed in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage with the Department of the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) on its bovine TB cattle vaccination project, including the development of a potential new skin test that can differentiate between cattle infected with bovine TB and those vaccinated for the disease.  Defra has recently announced further field trials and, while there are no plans to undertake similar trials in Northern Ireland, we take a very keen interest in the outcome of Defra’s work.  Currently, as the existing tuberculin skin test cannot differentiate between vaccinated and infected cattle, domestic and EU legislation prevents the vaccination of cattle for bovine TB.  Should Defra’s project succeed, it is expected that the approval of the new test by the World Organisation for Animal Health and the European Union would be needed before cattle vaccination for bovine TB could be considered for use in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that funding for special educational needs provision in mainstream schools is equitably distributed in rural areas.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my previous answer to AQW 21965/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 14th February 2025. All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements.  A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  All special provision pupils in mainstream schools attract the same level of funding under the CFF based on their phase of education, irrespective of the location of the school.The Funding Authority meets the full costs of staffing in special units.  In addition, schools receive a lump sum of £3,000 for each Specialist Provision Class.  As such, pupils with statements of SEN that are in attendance/registered in a special unit or special provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) attract a reduced Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) weighting.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the points discussed during any meetings they have held with the Consul General of China since the start of the 2022-2027 mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of applications for Department of Education representatives to join a school's Board of Governors are still waiting to be approved.
answer_text: Applications to become a DE school governor are always open. As Minister, I select and nominate/appoint candidates to DE school governor positions.As of 21 February 2024, there are 906 applicants who have applied and are not currently serving as a DE governor. As there are normally more applicants than DE governor positions, not all applicants can be appointed. Whilst applicants may specify a preference for a particular school(s) within their application, a post in any chosen school cannot be guaranteed. The reconstitution of all controlled and maintained schools is currently ongoing which includes over 700 DE governor positions. In addition to this, where resources permit, the Department has been reconstituting/filling its positions in other school types. This will continue throughout 2025/26 until all DE positions, where possible, are reconstituted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there has been any discussions in relation to compensation for families that lost perishable goods due to loss of electricity during Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: On 27 January 2025, I extended the scope of the Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) to provide financial assistance to councils and I have ring-fenced up to £1m to alleviate the impact of Storm Éowyn.However, under the Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance that I activated, the types of expenditure which are eligible includes the cost of overtime worked by council employees on the emergency, the cost of setting up temporary premises, the costs of providing emergency supplies of food and other emergency provisions during the period of the emergency. It will also include reimbursement of eligible expenditure to Voluntary & Community Organisations who have been working with councils to mitigate the effects of the emergency. The Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance that I activated, does not provide for payments to individual households.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps he has taken to increase awareness of online scams among vulnerable groups.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action his Department is taking to increase the capacity of Educational Psychologists.
answer_text: My department currently funds 38 student places on the Doctorate in Educational, Child and Adolescent Psychology (DECAP) course at Queen’s University Belfast (QUB).  Those completing the course are obliged to take up employment in the Education Authority’s (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) for at least three years after they qualify.  There are currently 94.06 full-time equivalent (FTE) Educational Psychologists and almost 36 FTE assistant Educational Psychologists in the EA.  It is expected that the number of Educational Psychologists will increase annually as students complete the DECAP course.A review of the time allocation model for access to Educational Psychology support is one of the actions to be taken forward by the EA as part of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action his Department is taking to increase early assessments for possible special educational needs.

Implementation of this initiative is subject to confirmation of Transformation funding.  My officials have commenced work with Health colleagues to develop the operational detail of this initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider introducing similar legislation to the Social Action, Responsibility and Heroism Act 2015 to mitigate fears around risk and liability that may prevent individuals from volunteering, aiding others or taking action in emergencies.
answer_text: I have no plans at present to introduce similar legislation to the Social Action, Responsibility and Heroism Act 2015. However, my Department is happy to receive representations and evidence in support of the need to progress such legislation in Northern Ireland in a future Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment he has made of the adequacy of the NIEA in addressing pollution incidents in the River Maine.
answer_text: Addressing water pollution is a priority for my Department and all reports are investigated. NIEA have responded to 163 reports of water pollution in the Braid and Maine (LMA) over the last three years with 56 of these confirmed as having had an impact on water quality. The NIEA Incident Hotline enables the public to report incidents of pollution at any time of the day. Prompt reporting is critical to a timely response to enable sufficient evidence to be gathered to take forward the most appropriate enforcement action.It is NIEA’s policy to pursue formal enforcement action where the polluter is identified and where the pollution has had a High or Medium severity environmental impact.I have allocated additional funding to NIEA from within my Department’s budget for work in the Lough Neagh catchment. This has supported the provision of additional officers to undertake catchment investigations, as well as increased inspection and enforcement activities. This work will include the inflowing rivers to the Lough, which includes the River Maine. I have also asked my officials to review fines and penalties for environmental crime, in collaboration with Department of Justice, to support this work.My commitment to improving water quality throughout Northern Ireland is set out in the recently published Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) which includes the requirement to publish a third cycle River Basin Management Plan (RBMP). I plan to bring the RBMP to the Executive in the coming weeks. The RBMP will contain a Programme of Measures, setting out the actions required to protect and improve all water bodies, including the River Maine. Delivery of the plan will require collective action across government, the public, the private sector and wider society.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is ensuring that all schools, regardless of their sector, receive equitable support services.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to support managing authorities and sectoral bodies across education, each with autonomy to tailor their support to their sector. The Independent Review of Education reflected on school management and support arrangements, highlighting the sub-optimal arrangements for controlled schools in particular. I have recently announced changes to address this matter, establishing dedicated structures in the interests of greater equity and consistency across our main education sectors. I want to see success across all of our school sectors, and I will always be open to further system changes where needed in future, including school management and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is ensuring that schools inform pupils regarding the skills needs gap within the local economy.
answer_text: Our education system aims to equip learners with the knowledge, skills and attributes that will support them to develop as individuals and as effective contributors to society and the economy.The needs of the economy and the skills required to sustain it are constantly changing and evolving. The system therefore needs to be adaptable and respond to feedback from employers.  Through the Entitlement Framework the range of subjects on offer to pupils, the mix of academic and vocational options and the range of extra-curricular choices equips learners with a more rounded set of skills and perspectives that are ideally suited to the flexible, evolving careers that will be inevitable over the next ten years and beyond.High quality careers education and guidance is crucial in ensuring learners are aware of the available pathways into higher education, further education, training and employment and the needs of employers. Schools are supported in this by the Careers Service who have partnership agreements with every post primary school in Northern Ireland. Employers themselves also have an important role to play in supporting work related learning through meaningful work experience and visits as well as in-school presentations and participation in careers fairs. To support this, I and my officials regularly engage with employers and employer organisations to promote and encourage their engagement with schools.As our economy develops, the demand for higher levels of skills and knowledge will continue to grow.  My department will continue to support as many young people as possible to reach the education and qualification levels that will offer viable and sustainable opportunities in our emerging labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department (i) is taking; and (ii) plans to take to help address homelessness affecting (a) children; and (b) families.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is currently implementing the Year 3 Action Plan of the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which can be found at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/ed2fb456-411c-409d-90dd-93d5fb387b09/Homelessness-Strategy-Year-3-Action-Plan.pdfIn addition to the action plans guiding the general delivery of the Homelessness Strategy, the Housing Executive published a Youth Homelessness Action Plan in December 2024 which can be found at:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/05fb0d2e-603f-4067-a381-04b2919b0ccd/Youth-Homelessness-Action-Plan-Accessible.pdf The Youth Homelessness Action Plan targets young people who: have been part of the care system prior to reaching 16 years of age, and then become homeless at a later point.are aged 16/17 years, not known to Social Services or who do not have care experience in their background, and present as homeless.are aged 18-25 years, who present to the Housing Executive as homeless.Of those children currently in temporary accommodation, 97% are in ‘own front door’ accommodation and, for those who are not, the Housing Executive will seek to identify alternative options at the earliest possible opportunity as part of its case management approach.The Housing Executive also provides support to a number of projects specific to young people such as:a Young Person’s Peer Support Project, delivered by the Northern Ireland Youth Forum,Housing First 4 Youth, delivered by Simon Community, andthe Shared Tenancies Project, delivered by MACS. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department defines and measure educational underachievement; and (ii) the steps being taken to ensure a comprehensive approach that moves beyond GCSE results.
answer_text: My Department accepts the definition of educational underachievement put forth within the ‘A Fair Start’ (AFS) report. The report states that underachievement “means that attainment is low, and lower than other comparison groups”.At system level, educational underachievement is currently primarily measured in relation to success at GCSE level. The baseline used most frequently by my Department is:5 + GCSEs at grades A*-C5 + GCSEs at grades A*-C, including English and mathsThis indicator has also been used within the Programme for Government’s Wellbeing Framework. Schools use a range of tools and measures to identify educational underachievement. Many schools use standardised tests to provide diagnostic assessment results annually. This baseline is used to track pupil progress and to inform learning strategies and curriculum decisions. Schools often map these outcomes against a measure of the child’s ability to identify underachievement.  My Department also funds a range programmes delivered by the Education Authority, voluntary and community sector and others, which utilise other metrics in relation to educational underachievement. These include, but are not limited to, Pupil Attitude to Self and School surveys, the CAT4 Test, Progress Test in English scores and Progress Test in Maths scores. We fully recognise the need for additional system level measures of educational achievement as we are currently without any national measures of performance at Key Stage 1, 2 and 3.  This is not acceptable and makes Northern Ireland at outlier internationally in being without basic performance data. My department has commenced a Review of Statutory Key Stage Assessment.  Recommendations for change will be brought forward to me for consideration to ensure that Northern Ireland has fit-for-purpose assessment processes which clearly identify educational underachievement throughout a child’s educational career.  I hope to announce proposals for the next three years in the near future.
