question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Executive agreed to return decision-making powers on aerodromes, including slot allocation, to the UK Parliament, as outlined in the Airports Slot Allocation (Alleviation of Usage Requirements etc.) Regulations 2025.
answer_text: Decision making powers on aerodromes, including slot allocated have always been reserved functions. On 7th August 2024 the Minister for Infrastructure wrote to the Executive to inform them that the Airports Slot Allocation (Alleviation of Usage Requirements etc.) Regulations 2025 would be extended to include the north. As per Devolution Guidance Notice 8 there is no formal requirement for agreement by the Assembly for subordinate legislation which extends to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department ensures that any funding provided effectively targets those that need it.
answer_text: My Department prioritises its funding with a clear focus on the Department of Education’s (DE) statutory obligations and policy objectives as outlined in Every CHILD DE Corporate Plan 2023-28 which in turn is guided and informed by my Department’s responsibilities under the Draft Programme for Government and commitments set out in the Children and Young People’s Strategy, A Fair Start, and the New Decade, New Approach agreement.  Within this framework, it is very much my vision that every child and young person is happy, learning and succeeding.However, I must stress that Education has been and remains significantly underfunded.  This is independently evidenced by both the Institute for Fiscal Studies in its last two comparison papers on school spending per pupil across the United Kingdom and more recently the Independent Review of Education’s report, Investing in a Better Future.Education continues to face unavoidable pay and cost pressures as well as rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations.  These are pressures neither the current nor next year’s budget fully address.Given the constrained capital budget for both 2024-25 and 2025-26, my Department continues to prioritise meeting existing contractual commitments, keeping schools open, children safe and providing places for children with Special Education Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his Department’s assessment of the effectiveness of the current policies aimed at tackling educational underachievement, including, (i) the Extended Schools Programme; (ii) Getting Ready to Learn; and (iii) the Pathway Fund.
answer_text: Policies such as Getting Ready to Learn (GRtL) and the Pathway Fund are aimed specifically at reducing underachievement from early childhood, while the Extended Schools Programme (ESP) is targeted at schools.The ESP aims to improve levels of educational achievement for disadvantaged children and young people by providing the additional support that they need to help them reach their full potential. Information on the ESP is collected via the Northern Ireland Extended Schools Information System (NIESIS) and used to monitor effectiveness with reports published annually.  In terms of effectiveness, improved attainment in literacy and numeracy is a key indicator of the success of the ESP. 89% of Extended Schools report ‘strong’ or ‘some’ evidence that their programmes improved attainment. To ensure that the ESP continues to meet the needs of disadvantaged children and young people, my Department has commissioned Stranmillis University College to undertake an independent evaluation of the ESP, the outcome of which will be used to inform future delivery of the programme. The evaluation will be completed in September 2025.The GRtL programme which supports DE-funded pre-school education settings, has established a universal approach to engage more effectively with parents to create, sustain and improve positive and nurturing home learning environments and develop the confidence and skills they need to ensure that their children grow up happy, healthy and achieve their full potential. In 2024/25, 75% of all pre-school settings are participating in the Getting Ready to Learn programme The benefits of the GRtL are evidenced through outcomes-based reporting carried out by the EA on an annual basis. A recent independent review commissioned by my Department, concluded that the Pathway Fund is an important component of holistic early years support. Reviewers recommended continuation of the Fund, reporting that it provides specific services for over 10,000 children annually who are at risk of educational underachievement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact of the Airports Slot Allocation (Alleviation of Usage Requirements etc.) Regulations 2025 on Northern Ireland's airports.
answer_text: Airports are categorised according to the level of demand/supply imbalance. Level 3 airports are those where demand significantly exceeds the airport capacity. The Airports Slot Allocation (Alleviation of Usage Requirements etc.) Regulations 2025 only relates to Level 3 airports. As there are currently no Level 3 airports in the North there will be no immediate impact in this jurisdiction. However, it is prudent that the legislation outlined extends to this jurisdiction as airports here may eventually reach Level 3 status. The regulations amend the definition of a new entrant airline and provides for alleviation for air carriers from complying with the 80:20 rule in the event of a future epidemic or pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether his Department provides any funding and administration for the Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) scheme; (ii) whether responsibility is shared between his Department and the Department for the Economy; (iii) whether funding is split between the Departments; and (iv) the role of his Department in relation to the EMA scheme.
answer_text: Whilst responsibility for the EMA scheme is shared between my Department  and the Department for Education (DE), the EMA budget sits with my Department. The policy in respect of EMA is a cross-cutting matter and is agreed between my Department and DE. My Department provides all necessary funding to the Student Loans Company (SLC) for the administration of the scheme and for the distribution of support to recipients, it also oversees the operation and governance of the scheme.

answer_text: As required by the legislation, the Department published the statutory report (the Section 10 Report) on Integrated Education on 26 April 2024. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department provides funding and administration for the Education Maintenance Allowance scheme; (ii) whether responsibility is shared between his Department and the Department for the Economy; (iii) whether funding is split between the departments; and (iv) the role of his Department in relation to the Education Maintenance Allowance scheme.
answer_text: The Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) scheme is a joint policy for which responsibility is shared between my Department and the Department for the Economy (DfE).The full costs of the EMA scheme are made up of a combination of financial support, which is paid directly to young people in the form of weekly allowances and bi-annual bonuses; and administration costs, which are paid to the Student Loans Company (SLC) who administer the EMA scheme on behalf of both Departments and to the education providers for administering the process locally. My Department holds a small budget to provide funding to schools every year, through the Education Authority, to assist them with the costs of administering the EMA scheme locally. DfE holds and manages the annual budget for the financial support paid directly to young people, for the administration costs in respect of the SLC and for payments to Further Education colleges for local administration. DfE is also responsible for the service level agreement and the relationship with SLC. My Department is responsible for policy matters and enquires relating to school pupils who are recipients of the EMA Scheme and for the administration of payments to schools through the EA, whilst DfE provide the equivalent for Further Education Colleges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) the establishment of a Nursery Unit at All Children’s Integrated Primary School, Newcastle; and (ii) Transformation to Controlled Integrated Status at (a) Ballyhenry Primary School; (b) Downshire Primary School; and (c) Culmore Primary School.
answer_text: The statutory objection period for Development Proposal (DP) 738 (A statutory nursery unit will be established at All Children’s Controlled Integrated Primary School to provide 26 part-time nursery places with effect from 1 September 2026, or as soon as possible thereafter) closed on 11 November 2024.  I am advised that officials are now assessing all relevant evidence and that a report will be sent to me for a decision on the proposal when that work is completed.  It is not possible to say when that work will be completed at this point.The statutory objection period for DP 736 (Ballyhenry Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter) closed on 12 November 2024.  The objection periods for DPs 735 (Downshire Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter) and 737 (Culmore Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter) both closed on 11 November 2024.  I am advised that officials are now assessing all relevant evidence and that reports for all three of these proposals will be sent to me for decisions when that work is completed.  It is not possible to say when that work will be completed at this point.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme; and (ii) when funding will be made available to schools.
answer_text: The Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme remains a priority for my Department.  A tender exercise is underway to procure 15,000 new desktop computers for issue to post primary and special schools directly at an estimated cost of £5.4m.Work is also underway to take forward the purchase of defibrillators for all schools which required them. A business case has been approved and guidance developed for the £4m outdoor learning equipment project.  Funding allocations will be made in the near future.  In terms of the pilot project to test the use of mobile phone inhibiting devices in ten post primary schools, a supplier will be appointed through established procurement processes.  It is anticipated that the successful supplier will be appointed in March 2025 with the pilot itself commencing in schools in September 2025.Work is ongoing in relation to longer-term investment that will bring forward improved accommodation and facilities to enable schools to better plan for and deliver the Physical Education (PE) curriculum effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when his Department plans to install Traffic Watch NI cameras in North Antrim; and (ii) whether the A26 (a) Frosses Road; (b) Crankill Road; and (c) Ballymena Road will be given special consideration for the installation of Traffic Watch NI cameras.
answer_text: My department is currently investigating the provision of traffic cameras at key locations on the road network across the northern division. As the A26 carries the highest volume of traffic in the North Antrim constituency it is envisaged that there will eventually be a number of cameras along its length.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for not having Traffic Watch NI cameras in operation in the North Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Traffic Watch NI cameras are mainly located where traffic volumes are greatest, or where congestion frequently occurs with the majority located in greater Belfast, Derry and along the strategic road network. Officials are investigating expanding this network however, progress has been limited due to the significant financial pressures and staff resourcing issues which we have faceddue to over a decade of underfunding and austerity by the British Government.I am informed that cameras have been erected on the Larne Road and Galgorm Road/North Road junction in Ballymena and the intention is to connect them to Traffic Watch NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any funding available to Ballymena Primary School to create an outdoor sensory area and garden.
answer_text: I am aware that Ballymena Primary School (PS) approached the Education Authority (EA) following my announcement of a curriculum-led capital programme aimed at supporting teaching and learning to ensure that every child in every school feels the benefits of capital investment through a range of low-impact, lower cost projects. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider introducing the Tell Us Once service in Northern Ireland, similar to what has been rolled out in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the recent outbreak of foot and mouth disease in Germany, to detail any measures in place to (i) reduce the risk of the virus entering Northern Ireland; and (ii) prevent its introduction to livestock.
answer_text: The emergence of Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) in Germany is of significant concern, not only for our livestock owners but also for the entire agricultural sector across the UK, Republic of Ireland and the EU.On notification of the outbreak in Germany my officials immediately carried out a tracing exercise to identify if any susceptible species, germplasm or products of animal origin had been imported to Northern Ireland from Germany within an established risk window.Furthermore, portal controls in line with the requirements laid down in legislation are implemented at points of entry to Northern Ireland.  These controls include identity and physical checks on consignments and the screening of passenger luggage and parcels arriving through airports.  In addition, DAERA implement a system of certification verification to ensure that animals and products arriving here have been certified in line with the required animal and public health attestations.My Department has well established robust disease control contingency plans in place to manage the risk of disease incursions and protect our farmers and food security. The FMD response plan outlines clear procedures for responding to suspect cases, including rapid diagnostic testing, protocols to restrict animal movements and the establishment of disease control zones to mitigate for onward spread of the disease. This plan was tested extensively during a UK national exercise in 2018. Communications reminding livestock keepers of the necessity of adhering to high levels of biosecurity are being regularly disseminated. In addition, veterinary practitioners and livestock keepers have been urged to remain alert to any unusual symptoms and report these immediately to a veterinarian or directly to DAERA.Officials are maintaining close and frequent communication with their counterparts across these islands to facilitate a comprehensive response. Finally, I updated my Ministerial colleagues at the Executive meeting on 16 January 2025 on the developments in Germany and informed all MLAs via a Written Ministerial Statement published 17 January 2025. This statement is available at https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/official-reports/written-ministerial-statements/2022---2027/bv169_daera-17012025.pdf and provides further information on the actions taken to date and future steps to be taken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any infrastructure project planned to increase the capacity of tourism in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The development of all infrastructure is critical to supporting a thriving tourism industry. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any collaborative work his Department and the Department for Infrastructure have carried out in relation to the publication of Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: My predecessor, Conor Murphy, invited the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) to nominate a representative to sit on the Tourism Partnership Board. This Board was established in September to advise on the formation of the Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan. I am grateful to DfI officials for playing an active role on the board.As outlined in the Vision, DfI’s work on developing transport plans, together with maintaining and strengthening our transport system, is crucial for meeting the travel experience needs of our visitors and citizens. A well-integrated and sustainable transport network not only improves accessibility to key attractions but also minimizes the environmental impact of travel. By future proofing and investing in our transport system, destinations can attract more visitors, reduce congestion, and promote local economies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of farm safety inspections carried out by his Department between April 2020 and March 2023.
answer_text: ‘Safety Essentials’ was an awareness raising campaign, developed as part of the Farm Safety Partnership’s 2020-2023 Action Plan, to provide guidance on how to reduce risk and encourage safe work practices and behaviours amongst the farming community. 	This awareness raising campaign was not developed as an enforcement initiative however, non-compliance where it was found in relation to key campaign messages was dealt with during inspections.  In the majority of cases, compliance was achieved through a variety of enforcement means including discussion and agreement, follow-up visits, formal letters and when required formal notices.		While not directly linked to the “Safety Essentials” campaign, during the period between 1 April 2020 to 31 March 2023 HSENI served a total of 79 enforcement notices on farms in the north requiring duty holders to address imminent risk of injury or comply with  relevant health and safety legislation. These predominantly served as a result of equipment maintenance and guarding issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he was consulted prior to Clause 56 of the Children's Wellbeing and Schools Bill being drafted.
answer_text: The Department has not been consulted about Clause 56.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the criteria that a charity shop must meet to be awarded rates relief.
answer_text: There is provision under Article 41 (5) of the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977 to distinguish a charity shop as exempt. This provision requires that:  The shop is occupied by a registered charity.The shop is used for the sale of donated goods*. The net profits are applied for the purposes of a charity. *In the event that there are also some “bought in” goods available for sale in the charity shop, the Net Annual Value (NAV) may be apportioned on the basis of relative turnover between donated and bought in goods.   Where charitable exemption does not apply the occupier may be eligible for small business rate relief provided it falls below the NAV threshold and meets criteria for the scheme.

These predominantly served as a result of equipment maintenance and guarding issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of potholes reported to his Department in the last six months; and (ii) how many of these reported potholes were repaired, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Data showing potholes reported by the public for the past several years is regularly published and available for public viewing through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/pothole-enquiries which can be downloaded and filtered by date, geographical area, road name, local district council, and status.Road surface defects are also published on the portal at:https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defectsSurface defects are recorded following routine safety inspections or ad-hoc site investigations by DfI staff mainly using hand-held field devices.  There are a range of defect categories used, including:  pothole, rutted, depressed, cracked, crazed uneven, edge deterioration etc., and a priority setting is based on the severity and risk to the public.  This data is can also be filtered by date, geographical area, road name, local district council, and status (e.g. completed).It is important to note that there can be a time lag between when each surface defect record is repaired and when this is updated on the maintenance management system.  Repair work is also only carried out in accordance with my Department’s current ‘Limited Service’ maintenance policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what regulations the Secretary of State for Education will have the powers to make under Clause 56 of the Children's Wellbeing and Schools Bill.
answer_text: The Department has not been consulted about Clause 56.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale behind his decision to close Balnamore Primary School at the end of the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: My rationale for the decision in relation to DP 731 Balnamore Primary which is now published on the Department’s website is that:-“Having carefully read, examined and considered all the papers provided to me including the significant number of objections to this proposal, it is clear to see there is a strong desire for Balnamore Primary School to remain. However, it is evident that this was once a successful school, but it has suffered a catastrophic decline in the number of children and young people attending the school. The analysis conducted shows that the significant sustainability challenges are irrecoverable. I note there are considerable available places in good alternative controlled schools in the area, with a declining school age population in the area I must conclude it would be in the best educational interest for those remaining children and young people to approve this proposal.I would like to acknowledge the efforts of those to try and rescue the school and I would ask the Education Authority to review this area and provide a comprehensive area solution and give urgent consideration as to how this well-equipped school with good accommodation and facilities can be repurposed to consolidate controlled provision in the area and other educational needs that may exist.I would also ask the Education Authority to ensure that the small number of children remaining, and their parents, are supported through the transition to other schools in the area. Every effort should be made to minimise the disruption to children’s educational experiences and support, care and attention should be given to affected teaching and non-teaching staff who are impacted by this closure.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support his Department and the Educational Authority gives to pupils, parents and staff at schools that are closing at the end of the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The discontinuance of any school is a difficult time for a school community.  When a school is closed, the Education Authority (EA) and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools where appropriate, are responsible for supporting the affected families both in terms of ensuring that they secure an alternative school placement in the area and in terms of ensuring that any additional educational needs that those pupils have, are also met in their new school.The EA, as the managing authority for Controlled schools, has a guidance and support role in the discontinuance of approved Controlled school closures.  It is their responsibility within statutory regulations, to ensure staff, parents and pupils are supported throughout the discontinuance.  The EA has confirmed that its officers work collaboratively with local schools to ensure that the transfer is managed with minimal disruption to the pupils.  It is essential for pupils that their education experience is seamless.  The Principal of the school is a vital link in the process, and throughout, support and guidance are provided.  EA Human Resources officers and union representatives provide group and individual support for all staff members.The EA operates an internal Cross Directorate Implementation Group, of which the Principal is a member, to manage the operational aspects of a discontinuance.  This group will be reconvened to assist with the recently announced decisions.If there is a need to provide places at other schools in an area to facilitate a school closure, the Department will intervene to provide a temporary variation to school numbers to ensure all pupils are placed in a suitable school within a reasonable travelling distance of their home address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the options available to him in the event of a bird flu outbreak in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI), commonly known as bird flu, is listed as a Category A disease under EU Regulation 2016/429, the Animal Health Law.Category A diseases do not normally occur in the EU or Northern Ireland (NI) and require immediate eradication measures be taken as soon as they are detected.In the event of an outbreak of HPAI in NI, disease control actions taken by my Department are mandatory, as legislated for in EU and domestic law.  Initial actions include, restricting suspect premises, conducting veterinary investigations, taking samples from susceptible species for testing at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute, establishing disease control zones, the humane culling of birds on an infected premises and cleansing and disinfection of an infected premises.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of road salt his Department currently holds.
answer_text: This winter, my Department has used 45,840 tonnes of salt to date , with 25,250 tonnes of salt remaining in our depots and another 20,000 tonnes of contingency salt available in strategic reserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether organised fireworks displays must take into account their proximity to animal welfare establishments when submitting a request to stage such a display.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland a valid fireworks licence is required to purchase, possess and use fireworks.  The Department will only issue a licence when it has been deemed safe to do so.  The location and surrounding area is checked for all applications and will include any nearby animal welfare establishments.A fireworks licence includes conditions on use, including date and time restrictions on when the fireworks can be used.  Where circumstances require, a fireworks licence may include a condition that local residents, local landowners, farmers and animal welfare establishments are informed in advance of the firework display.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of recorded antisocial behaviour incidents that include a firework code incident that have taken place since 2020.
answer_text: The recording of information in relation to anti-social behaviour incidents that have occurred is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there are any plans to align PSNI maternity payment with England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: My officials are considering a business case seeking alignment with maternity and parental leave allowances for PSNI officers with those for officers in England and Wales.  The Business Case must satisfy the requisite approvals process, including approval by the Department of Finance.  A Departmental Determination will then be required to implement any new arrangements.   The terms for police officers in Scotland are determined under separate arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional consideration is he giving to rural schools when considering a 20mph speed limit.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been completed and is available on the Department’s website:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schoolsThe review recommends that the Department should provide a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The review report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work updating our policy document is underway. While I would be keen to have balance between rural and urban schools, I think it is more important that those schools in greatest need of safety measures are treated as this will achieve the best outcomes in terms of road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) schools; and (ii) roads at which he is considering introducing a 20mph speed limit.
answer_text: Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools will be included within budget planning considerations for next financial year and until this is confirmed I am not in a position to commit to specific schemes.You will also be interested to hear that as part of my consideration of the review of the part-time 20mph speed limit at schools initiative, I have asked officials to prepare a paper on 20mph speed limits in general, which is in progress.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how their Department is working with other stakeholders to proactively address violence against women and girls.
answer_text: Implementation of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls will require all parts of society and government working together. Through the Local Change Fund, TEO are working directly with Councils to facilitate engagement with local stakeholders to collectively address violence against women and girls. Engagement with key EVAWG Experts in the Community and Voluntary Sector is ongoing through the Regional Change Fund. Work is also progressing in collaboration with partners to advance other elements of the Delivery Plan including working with sectoral groups to address key issues such as Safer Socialising and Healthy Relationships and supporting technological and other innovations by the private sector to improve the safety of women and girls.An Insight and Reference Network will evolve from and continue the collaborative focus of the co-design process which shaped the Strategic Framework. It will give strong connection to and support collaboration with the Community and Voluntary sector, and other partners. It will promote the work and impact of these organisations, share learning, forge new connections, and provide a mechanism to inject service provision expertise and lived experience into delivery of the programme.A key objective of the Strategic Framework is the development of a whole of government approach to Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.  Work is progressing at pace across departments and their agencies in the development of a coordinated approach including the development of training for all staff, and targeted interventions in other key areas such as housing and health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19953/22-27, to detail the work his officials are carrying out to work collaboratively with the Education Authority to improve the school transport service.
answer_text: Providing home to school transport for over 90,000 eligible pupils each day is a large operational undertaking and my officials continually work closely with colleagues in the Education Authority (EA) to discuss improvements to the delivery of school transport. As well as regular contact seeking assurances and resolution to issues arising around transport for specific pupils, schools or routes, there are a number of ongoing projects to enhance delivery of services. These include the EA fleet replacement programme and capital funding was awarded in October 2024 to fund the purchase of new buses. In addition, capital funding was also awarded in October to enable the EA to extend a pilot programme trialling smaller 7-seat vehicles within the EA transport fleet to provide more flexibility in the delivery of school transport, especially in relation to pupils with additional transport requirements due to Special Educational Needs or Disabilities. My officials are also currently working with the EA in finalising a business case for the digitisation of key transport functions which will enable the transport service to track vehicles progress in real time, improve route planning and optimise the school transport network. This should deliver efficiencies and improve the transport service for children. My Department will continue to support the EA and work closely with it in the development of any such initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the number of police officers staffing the domestic violence unit in the north west being cut by 50 per cent, whether there are any plans to reduce officers staffing other similar units across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The allocation of policing resources is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the selection process for the appointment of the next chairperson of the board of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The selection process was conducted in accordance with the Code of Practice issued by the Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland (CPA Northern Ireland).The competition launched on 12 September 2024, inviting those interested in applying to complete an application form. Applicants were expected to demonstrate evidence (in their application and at interview) that they had the necessary experience, skills and competencies, to meet six essential criteria, which were as follows:Customer Focus / ServiceStrategic LeadershipFinance and Transformational Change ManagementGovernanceCo-operation and CollaborationCommunicationWritten applications were then considered by an assessment panel, with only those candidates who provided evidence of sufficient quality to meet the eligibility criteria invited to interview.Following the interviews the assessment panel provided an unranked list of four candidates who were considered suitable for appointment. In doing so they provided a candidate summary based on the information provided by each applicant at interview.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department plans to review the Home to School transport policy, which was last updated in 2009.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. The transport policy underpins other policies within the department such as  school attendance and is a key support for many pupils with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND). Officials are currently developing  an action plan arising from the Landscape Review of the Education Authority (EA) alongside my considerations of the proposals outlined within the SEND Reform Agenda, both of which include consideration of operational delivery of services such as school transport.  Any review of the Home to School Transport Policy should complement and build on the outcomes from these workstreams and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my Department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision. The cost of delivering the current school transport service for over 90,000 pupils is significant at over £120 million in 2023/24. Any proposals for policy change would need to be carefully scoped out and costed within the context of a detailed policy review and would likely require changes to legislation. In the interim my officials will continue to work collaboratively with the EA on continuous improvement in transport service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to introduce a programme to address educational underachievement in rural areas.
answer_text: I recognise the need to address educational underachievement wherever it exists, including in rural areas.  For this reason, the new RAISE Programme will apply in selected disadvantaged areas across in Northern Ireland.For disadvantaged communities in rural areas such as Millisle, Irvinestown, Lisnaskea and Bessbrook the RAISE Programme presents an exciting opportunity to tackle educational disadvantage and embed innovative ways of collaborative working between schools, communities, families and wider partners.The Programme will enable the development and delivery of a bespoke Strategic Area Plan for each Locality.  This will be co-designed by schools and other settings, families/carers and others with a valuable role to play in supporting children and young people’s learning in the locality.Engagement is currently underway with key stakeholders serving children and young people in the area.  This will inform the Strategic Area Plan by identifying the specific problems they are facing locally.  The Plan will set out a range of actions to achieve the strategic objectives of raising achievement and addressing educational disadvantage, and also of raising aspiration and increasing the value of a whole community approach to education.Ultimately this will help to deliver on the mission set by the report ‘A Fair Start’ to ensure all children and young people regardless of background are given the best start in life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of candidates that were considered before he made his decision on the appointment of the next chairperson of the board of the Education Authority.
answer_text: A total of 16 applications were received for chairperson of the board of the Education Authority. Following a sift of applications and interviews, the assessment panel recommended four candidates to me as suitable for appointment, having met all six essential criteria for the position of chairperson of the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide (i) a list; and (ii) the circumstances of all Ministerial Directions he has issued since February 2024.
answer_text: No ministerial directions have been issued since February 2024

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider leaving larger and more frequent salt piles along roads that are not eligible to be gritted by his Department.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 52,500 grit piles and 5,750 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, subject to meeting the required criteria in my Department’s Winter Service Policy.This policy states the recommended size and spacing of grit piles based on severity of road gradient.  Gradient steeper than 1 in 10: 	provide 150-200kg grit pile, 25m to 30m apartGradient between 1 in 20 to 1 in 10: 	provide 200-250kg grit pile, 30m to 50m apart

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the actions his Department is taking to ensure that every child, who is entitled to free schools meals, is receiving a hot nutritious meal while at school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has a number of contingencies in place when disruption to its normal hot school meal service occurs in a school and these are considered in the following order:Meals can be transported from another school with a production kitchen if it has sufficient capacity;Prepared meals can be brought into the school, providing there are sufficient staff and oven capacity available to heat/ serve the meals;A packed lunch service can be offered to all pupils, subject to the capacity of the external provider;Packed lunches can be provided for pupils entitled to free school meals only, subject to the capacity of the external provider.The EA continues to prioritise the preparation and serving of meals in a safe environment, meeting food safety regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action his Department is taking to ensure rural roads in North Antrim are adequately supplied with salt piles.
answer_text: My Department provides over 5,500 salt bins and 58,000 grit piles annually for the public to use on a self-help basis.  Officials in my Department renewed the grit piles along the rural road network, including North Antrim, in advance of the current winter service season.Grit piles can be replenished upon request, once they become depleted, subject to the availability of resources. Requests can be made using the online reporting tool: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/report-issue-ice-or-snow

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the advice he would provide to residents of Fernbrook, Kells, whose requests for the installation of a grit box have been refused.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.Members of the public can report issues with ice or snow on the road network using the NI Direct website: Report an issue with ice or snow | nidirect. The website also provides useful information and links to other sources of information on dealing with snow and ice conditions including my Department’s Winter Service Information Leaflet.I can also advise that the Highway Code provides useful advice for driving in wintry weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any contingency measures in place for canteen staff sickness and shortages.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has a number of contingencies in place when disruption to school meal provision occurs and these are considered in the following order:Meals can be transported from another school with a production kitchen, subject to capacity constraints.Prepared meals can be brought into the school, providing there are sufficient staff and oven capacity available to heat/ serve the meals.A packed lunch service can be offered to all pupils, subject to the capacity of the external provider.Packed lunches can be provided for pupils entitled to free school meals only, subject to the capacity of the external provider.The EA continues to prioritise the preparation and serving of meals in a safe environment, ensuring food safety regulations are met.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how their Department plans to engage and involve young people in policy development.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline any support available to assist with maintenance to the exterior of a school building.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the work of the IMPACTAgewell project in the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council.
To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in North Antrim presented as homeless over the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefit of multi-year funding on workforce planning for the health and social care system.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made in growing the HSC workforce over recent years with the number of whole time equivalent directly employed staff having increased by 16.0% (+9,063) since 2018.  However, my Department’s ongoing strategic workforce planning has identified the need for further and sustained increase investment in pre-registration and post-registration training to develop the workforce of the right size and with the right skills to serve our population into the future.  Unfortunately, the current pattern of one-year Departmental funding cycles seriously constrains the assessment of affordability for long-term investment in education and training in a context where there are always competing claims on finite financial resources.  This limitation is particularly relevant given the multi-year nature of many training programmes across the healthcare professions. Introduction of multi-year funding would also provide a range of other benefits.  It would bring much needed clarity, certainty and stability into the local health education system.  Education providers would be in a better position to make the necessary long-term investment in providing training capacity; much of which cannot be simply turned on and off.  The health and social care system would also be in a much better place to create and sustain the quality trainee placement opportunities necessary to support the development of professional skills.  Health and social care organisations would have a clearer understanding of the future supply of qualified healthcare professionals enabling them to implement plans for service transformation. Finally, it would enable employers to implement career pathways that are crucial to the recruitment and retention of healthcare professionals across health and social care. That is how strategic workforce planning should work.  Unfortunately, the current funding arrangements impede the ability to think strategically and plan effectively.Every day I respond to questions or correspondence from health professionals, the public and their representatives, including members of this Assembly, asking me to address the challenges facing the health and social care workforce.  These issues are a priority for me too. My vision is one where we are to make the optimum long-term actions to develop the health and social care workforce.  Multi-year funding in support of workforce planning would assist significantly in achieving this objective.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the work his Department is undertaking to further encourage women and girls' involvement in science, technology, engineering and mathematics.
answer_text: I am committed to providing equal access to the curriculum for all pupils.Specifically in the area of STEM and with a growing demand for people who are well-qualified in these subjects, it is vital that they are offered and delivered in our schools.  The uptake in STEM subjects has remained steady.  In the 2022/23 academic year, 39% of GCSE examination entries were in STEM subjects; 41% for males and 36% for females. At A-Level, the figure is 37% with 45% for males and 31% for females.  This is very encouraging given the wide and varied array of subjects on offer to our students.My Department works with the Department for the Economy on initiatives to support the development of skills.  An example of this is the 14-19 Framework “Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14-19 Education and Training: A Framework to Transform 14-19 Education and Training Provision” which recognises that gendered patterns emerge in STEM related professions. The Framework contains strategic actions focusing on equitable work experience and reviewing the model of careers education and guidance in schools to ensure that our children and young people can make informed choices.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline the number of children with a statement of special educational needs who are still awaiting an individual learning assistant, in North Antrim.
